Always verify current code state against error plans to avoid investigating already-resolved issues.

kill_agent() now accepts _context parameter. When an agent self-invokes kill_agent(run_id=...), the function compares _context.session_id against the agent's child_session_id; matching sessions trigger self-termination logic (returns 'success' instead of 'cancelled'). Subtle behavior requiring explicit context passing for self-termination detection.

safe_evaluator.visit_Name() was modified to accept lowercase boolean/null literals (true, false, none) in addition to Python's standard True, False, None. This enables evaluation of JSON-formatted boolean values. 7 unit tests added validating lowercase literal handling.

kill_agent(run_id=...) self-termination requires _context parameter check comparing _context.session_id against agent.child_session_id; self-termination status should default to 'success' not 'cancelled'
safe_evaluator's visit_Name() parser only accepted Python-style True/False/None; extended to accept lowercase true/false/none variants for JSON/general expression compatibility

kill_agent self-termination detection: works via both session_id path (resolved_session_id is not None) and run_id path (_context.session_id == agent_session_id). The _context parameter is injected by the tool registry call flow.
.gobby/ sync file changes from post-commit task/memory operations don't require separate commits; they are automatically picked up by the next session per migration system protocol.

Gobby stop hook 'require-error-triage' requires verification that lint (ruff check) and tests passed on modified files before task closure. Verify results or run verification if not done, then set pre_existing_errors_triaged = true to clear the gate.

Post-commit sync file mutations in `.gobby/` directory are expected behavior from post-commit hook operations (task/memory persistence). These changes do not require a separate git commit; they are automatically picked up by the next session's commit workflow.
_notifications.py contains mypy type errors (verified via mypy scan). heartbeat.py, runner.py, and isolation.py are clean with no type violations and require no type annotation fixes.
gobby-tasks MCP proxy can be used to create and manage tasks when native task creation tools are unavailable or blocked.

CodexAppServerClient integration pattern (App-Server v2): (1) Add codex_app_server: bool = False to config in src/gobby/config/app.py; (2) In src/gobby/runner.py, instantiate CodexAppServerClient when flag enabled AND codex CLI available, pass to HTTPServer; (3) In src/gobby/http.py, call start() before adapter attachment and stop() in shutdown cleanup.

In src/gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py, input parameters are normalized from kebab-case to snake_case: thread-id→thread_id, input-messages→input_messages, last-assistant-message→last_assistant_message, turn-id→turn_id. Return dict also includes cwd and turn_id fields.

Codex app-server v2 integration architecture: (1) config flag codex_app_server: bool = False in app.py for opt-in, (2) conditional CodexAppServerClient instantiation in runner.py when config enabled AND codex CLI available in PATH, (3) lifecycle management via start() before adapter attachment and stop() during shutdown cleanup in http.py. Pattern demonstrates proper external client integration.

Hook dispatcher parameter normalization in hook_dispatcher.py: _normalize_input_data() now accepts kebab-case parameter variants (thread-id, input-messages, last-assistant-message, turn-id) in addition to snake_case. Return dict extended to include cwd and turn_id fields. The os.getcwd() fallback removed from adapter.py (was incorrectly returning daemon working directory instead of task context) and now returns None with warning.

Codex CLI sends notify payloads with kebab-case field names (thread-id, turn-id, input-messages, last-assistant-message, cwd). The _normalize_input_data() function in hook_dispatcher.py now handles all three naming conventions (snake_case, camelCase, kebab-case). The CodexNotifyAdapter no longer falls back to os.getcwd() for missing cwd — it returns None instead, since the daemon's cwd is never the project directory. The codex_app_server config flag in DaemonConfig controls whether CodexAppServerClient is created in runner.py. CodexAdapter.EVENT_MAP includes thread/closed for session end events.

In the MCP proxy system, `call_context` (containing session_id, run_id, project_id, token) flows from `fire_continuation()` through `registry.call(tool_name, arguments, call_context=call_context)` in `src/gobby/mcp_proxy/services/tool_proxy.py` and becomes accessible to tools as `_context`. This allows tools to access execution context without explicit parameter passing.

Continuation firing has two paths: (1) heartbeat in `execution_manager.py` touches `updated_at` for alive agents, and (2) `fire_continuation` is called from completion handling. The `kill_agent` function and `call_context` flow through `registry.call(tool_name, args, context=call_context)` where `call_context` includes `session_id`. Tool context (`_context`) is set via `_resolve_and_set_project_context` in the MCP proxy services layer at `src/gobby/mcp_proxy/services/tool_proxy.py`.

Event type additions to CodexAdapter require two coordinated code changes: (1) add event to SESSION_TRACKING_EVENTS configuration, (2) add mapping case in translate_to_hook_event() function. Both changes must complete together for proper event handling. Phase 3a implemented this pattern for thread/closed event.

The app-server (`src/gobby/servers/`) implements a JSON-RPC protocol for Codex communication. The client side is in `src/gobby/codex/app_server_client.py`. HTTP server setup is in `src/gobby/servers/http.py` with ServiceContainer injection.

Task state 'needs_review' automatically triggers Claude Opus QA agent spawn as next phase in autonomous workflow. Pattern: dev completion → continuation → QA review → approval/reopening → final continuation → completion. No manual intervention required between phases.
QA agent automatically spawns and executes when a task reaches 'needs_review' status in the continuation-based pipeline orchestration flow

Infrastructure/orchestration test tasks should not be directly implemented. When a task is framed as 'spawn an agent via dev-loop pipeline to implement Task X' or similar, the goal is to test agent spawning/execution capability, not complete Task X. Task X serves as test payload and should remain open for the spawned agent to execute—implementing it directly eliminates the test case and wastes the infrastructure validation opportunity.

dispatch_batch parameter forwarding in src/gobby/mcp_proxy/tools/spawn_agent/_factory.py is already implemented (all 24 tests passing); the actual dev-loop pipeline test for live heartbeat mechanism validation remains incomplete

Mypy type errors in src/gobby/pipeline_heartbeat.py blocked heartbeat functionality; now resolved. heartbeat.py, runner.py, and isolation.py are clean with no type violations. _notifications.py still contains mypy type errors.

@registry.tool decorator auto-generates MCP schemas from function signatures. Adding new parameters to a tool function's signature automatically updates the schema without manual intervention. Verified in dispatch_batch() implementation (src/gobby/mcp_proxy/tools/spawn_agent/_factory.py, lines 130-183).

@registry.tool decorator auto-generates MCP schemas from function signatures - when adding parameters to a tool function, the schema updates automatically without manual schema file edits. This pattern applies to spawn_agent tools and other MCP proxy tools.

@registry.tool decorator auto-generates MCP schemas from function signatures using Python type annotations. When adding parameters to spawn_agent factory functions, include proper type hints in the function signature and the schema updates automatically—no manual schema modification required.
MCP schema description for dispatch_batch is generic enough to cover isolation context parameters without needing parameter-specific schema documentation updates

Gemini CLI has upstream bug with Git worktrees (GitHub #12050): expects `.git/` as directory but worktrees use `.git` as file. Not actionable on Gobby side. Workaround: use clones for Gemini testing environments instead of worktrees.

Sessions table does NOT have a `cwd` column (verified in BASELINE_VERSION: 146). Sessions reference project via project_id foreign key; working directory path lives in projects table. No per-session working directory override exists.
No merge-back mechanism exists between dev completion phase and QA dispatch phase in dev-loop pipeline - blocks clone-based isolation strategy

Gobby's require-task-close stop hook enforces that task #9918 must be transitioned via close_task() before commit succeeds. Hook blocks commit with 'in_progress' status; both commit AND close_task() orchestration call are required to close a task.

The Gobby project enforces a 'require-error-triage' hook rule that mandates verification that lint and tests passed on all touched/modified files before commits can be landed. This is a blocking requirement that must be satisfied in the workflow.
claude.json writes now use atomic operations with JSONDecodeError exception handling to prevent corruption on write failures
Migration v146 adds composite indexes for query optimization; baseline schema requires audit per established protocol (completed in v0.2.28 release)

rule_engine.py has _clear_edit_write_state(variables) helper that sets edit_write_pending=False and edit_write_stop_blocks=0. Used at 3 sites: circuit breaker tripped, no-rules after_tool success, with-rules after_tool success. Keeps the clearing logic DRY.

Gobby's `require-task-close` stop hook rule enforces that all claimed/active tasks must be in terminal states (`closed` or `escalated`) before process shutdown completes. If this rule blocks the stop sequence, the blocking task must be transitioned to `closed` or `escalated`.

get_app_context() singleton accessor created in app_context.py and wired into runner.py to resolve type annotation issues. Centralizes app context access for modules like _notifications.py that need context without circular imports.
app_context.py: singleton get_app_context accessor; runner.py: wires as dependency injection to break circular import with _notifications.py

Pre-commit hook 'require-task-close' in hook_dispatcher.py blocks commits when task is in 'in_progress' status. Task must be transitioned to 'closed' or 'review_approved' state to satisfy the hook and allow commit to proceed.
CLI `cron add` command at cli/cron.py:70 missing 'handler' in click.Choice list, though backend fully supports handler jobs. This is a CLI-only constraint blocking user-facing handler job creation.
agent_definition parameter not yet wired into _execute_agent_spawn() in handler flow—identified as Phase 4 prep work, currently non-blocking but needed for agent spawning through cron handlers.

ChatSession (keyed by conversation_id, wrapping ClaudeSDKClient) is the correct foundation for conductor; conductor operates as a chat session receiving messages from cron via WebSocket instead of HTTP, not a separate epic.

Conductor glue code reduced to ~50 lines (~90% ready); uses pub/sub architecture where cron broadcasts events via WebSocket and conductor subscribes; existing infrastructure already supports parametric subscriptions like 'cron_event:job_name=pipeline-heartbeat'

Conductor implementation uses pub/sub WebSocket model with ChatSession, leveraging existing parametric subscription infrastructure (e.g., 'cron_event:job_name=pipeline-heartbeat'). HTTP endpoint approach and conversation_id targeting requirement removed. Reduces conductor to ~50 lines of glue code with no additional abstractions needed.

Conductor technical gaps identified but not yet prioritized: error handling strategy for failed ticks, event replay mechanics for recovery, and subscription cleanup on conductor disconnect. Committed to roadmap addition.

tool_block_pending state in rule_engine.py should clear unconditionally on ANY successful AFTER_TOOL event, not just when the blocked tool itself succeeds. Fix applied at lines ~220 (early-return path) and ~250 (main evaluation path). This is a key architectural decision for block state management.
CronScheduler.on_run_complete callback is wired to broadcast_cron_event() via WebSocket for real-time cron event dissemination to connected clients

ConductorManager implements lazy ChatSession initialization (deferred to first tick), idle timeout-based teardown to prevent resource leaks, and skip-if-busy guard to prevent concurrent conductor overlaps

Phase 4 (Persistent Conductor + Cron Broadcast) landed in commit c15c6d2c on branch 0.2.28. Key components:

- ConductorConfig at src/gobby/config/conductor.py (enabled, model, tick_interval_seconds, idle_timeout_seconds, skip_if_busy)
- ConductorManager at src/gobby/conductor/manager.py — lazy ChatSession init, idle timeout teardown, skip-if-busy, error recovery. Implements CronHandler interface (__call__) for direct registration with CronExecutor.
- System cron job 'gobby:conductor-tick' auto-created when conductor.enabled=true
- CronScheduler.on_run_complete callback for event broadcasting (src/gobby/scheduler/scheduler.py)
- setup_cron_event_broadcasting() in runner_broadcasting.py
- CLI cron add now accepts 'handler' action type (was missing)
- agent_definition resolution in _execute_agent_spawn: resolves via resolve_agent(), prepends preamble, uses agent provider as fallback
- YAML templates: conductor.yaml, spawn-developer.yaml, spawn-qa.yaml
- Deferred items doc: docs/plans/conductor-deferred.md (notifications, observability, ~600 lines cleanup)

tool_block_pending clearing logic must maintain scope awareness - only clear when the same tool that created the block completes, not when ANY tool succeeds. Regression pattern: check scope matching in block clearing conditions to prevent premature gate clearing.

Runner tests fail when AgentLifecycleMonitor async cleanup methods execute against mocked database connections. Fix: Add AsyncMock patch for AgentLifecycleMonitor in create_base_patches() function to prevent async operations on mocked connections.

AgentLifecycleMonitor async cleanup hitting mocked DB in tests: add AsyncMock patch for AgentLifecycleMonitor to create_base_patches() function to prevent lifecycle methods from accessing mocked database

In conductor session _handle_tick() method, idle detection checks via _ensure_session() must execute BEFORE _last_activity = now assignment. Reordering prevents stale timestamps causing idle timeout detection to malfunction
DoneEvent constructor in conductor tests requires tool_calls_count parameter - must be provided when instantiating DoneEvent in test helpers, or tests will fail with missing argument error

Web UI frontend is in `web/` directory, uses Vite + Vitest (v4.x), package name `@gobby/web-ui`. Run tests with `cd web && npx vitest run`. TypeScript configured with `web/tsconfig.json` and `web/tsconfig.node.json`.

Phases 1-3 completed in commit 3c05c266: handler action type support, 60-second pipeline heartbeat with auto system job creation, and cron E2E with 7 integration tests. New utility function get_job_by_name() added.

Phase 1 of gobby-cron scheduling refactor: Add `handler` action type to CronExecutor class with callback registry pattern. Daemon registers internal handlers during init, enabling cron jobs to invoke daemon-internal code via callbacks rather than executing jobs directly.
GobbySpanProcessor enriches spans with Gobby context (session_id, task_id, agent_id) via ContextVars to avoid thread-local state coupling.
LangWatch instrumentation uses OpenLLMetry instrumentors for anthropic, openai, and google-genai to capture zero-instrumentation LLM telemetry (model, tokens, latency, cost).
LangWatch operates as an OTLP endpoint receiver, not a proprietary library. Integration uses OpenTelemetry v2 framework with OTLP/HTTP export.

LangWatch integration is OTLP endpoint configuration, not library integration. Real scope: add LLM auto-instrumentors (OpenLLMetry packages) to existing OTel v2 plan, not standalone LangWatch library integration.

LLM instrumentation architecture for OTel v2: OpenLLMetry auto-instrumentor packages (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) capture LLM calls (model, tokens, latency, cost) → GobbySpanProcessor enriches spans with Gobby context (session_id, task_id, agent_id) via ContextVars → OTLP/HTTP export to LangWatch endpoint (SaaS or self-hosted)

As of 2026-03-08: Cron scheduler supports invoking Python callables via handler action type. Heartbeat detects stalled pipeline state through pure DB queries only. Conductor integration (Phase 4) landed in commit c15c6d2c on branch 0.2.28.

Token consumption occurs exclusively in Phase 4+ when ChatSession is instantiated with haiku model (conductor layer). Conductor not yet implemented. Daemon/cron/heartbeat run continuously at zero tokens; conductor would burn tokens on explicit instantiation and per tick.

PipelineHeartbeat.__call__ is a pure Python function (not an agent) invoked by cron scheduler's asyncio loop within daemon process. Zero token consumption—only database queries. Heartbeat and conductor/agent are architecturally separate concerns.

In Gobby, LLM token consumption only occurs when _pipeline_rerun_callback fires—specifically when a pipeline stalls or the agent becomes unresponsive. Heartbeat handler (DB queries only) and cron scheduler loop (asyncio.sleep(10) polling) consume zero tokens during idle state.
Heartbeat auto-creates cron job named `gobby:pipeline-heartbeat` on daemon startup. Auto-initialization verified by integration tests (27 total passing).

Pipeline heartbeat implementation plan detailed in `docs/plans/pipeline-heartbeat.md` with Phases 1-4 complete. Phase 4 (Persistent Conductor + Cron Broadcast) landed in commit c15c6d2c. Deferred items documented at docs/plans/conductor-deferred.md.

Added get_stalled_executions() method to LocalPipelineExecutionManager (runner.py) to support pipeline heartbeat execution state detection. Query this method when implementing pipeline monitoring features.
Pipeline heartbeat auto-creates system cron job on startup in pipeline_heartbeat.py. If debugging cron initialization or system job creation, check this auto-registration behavior.

Pipeline heartbeat queries are cross-project (system-level safety net) even though cron jobs themselves are per-project with project_id and project-scoped UI. This architectural decision allows heartbeat to detect/recover stalled executions globally.

Heartbeat handler must query stalled RUNNING executions with all_projects=True in get_stalled_executions() because scheduling infrastructure is per-project but the agent's job is to manage pipelines across all projects. This is the architectural rationale for cross-project querying.

Pipeline heartbeat system auto-creates a system cron job named 'gobby:pipeline-heartbeat' with 60-second interval on startup (in runner.py or pipeline_heartbeat.py). This drives the health check across stalled executions.

CronExecutor handler action type: registered via `cron_executor.register_handler(name, async_callable)`. Handler name referenced in `action_config["handler"]`. System cron jobs use `gobby:` prefix convention (e.g., `gobby:pipeline-heartbeat`). PipelineHeartbeat queries across ALL projects (`all_projects=True`) because the daemon is single-instance serving all projects. Heartbeat auto-created on startup if missing via `cron_storage.get_job_by_name()`. Key files: `src/gobby/scheduler/executor.py` (CronHandler type + register_handler), `src/gobby/workflows/pipeline_heartbeat.py` (PipelineHeartbeat class), `src/gobby/storage/cron.py` (get_job_by_name), `src/gobby/storage/pipelines.py` (get_stalled_executions with all_projects flag).

Heartbeat's get_stalled_executions() was incorrectly scoped to self.project_id, causing pipeline stalls in non-home projects to go undetected. Fix: use all_projects=True parameter to query stalled executions across all projects regardless of daemon's home project.
System runs ONE daemon instance for all projects, not per-project daemons. One database, multiple projects. This architecture determines how monitoring and stalling detection must be implemented.
Cron job test fixtures enforce minimum check_interval of 10 minutes; intervals shorter than 10 minutes cause test failures. Required for fixture correction before merge.

Architectural note: Gobby runs a single daemon for all projects. GobbyRunner is per-project but system-level agents (PipelineHeartbeat, Conductor) operate across ALL projects. Conductor now implemented in Phase 4 with ConductorManager.

Gobby runs a single daemon for all projects (one DB at ~/.gobby/gobby-hub.db). Cron scheduling is per-project: CronJobStorage.create_job() includes project_id, list_jobs() accepts project_id filter, web UI is keyed to project. However, system-level agents like PipelineHeartbeat and the future conductor operate across ALL projects — heartbeat uses get_stalled_executions(all_projects=True) to detect stalled pipelines regardless of which project they belong to.
Heartbeat cron job fixture requires check_interval ≥10 minutes; this constraint was corrected in test fixtures and affects job scheduling behavior.

Conductor pipeline architectural gap: No multi-project pipeline push capability exists. Each GobbyRunner instance binds to single project with per-runner cron scheduler. Affects heartbeat job scheduling and requires architectural change for multi-project support.
System/internal cron jobs should use `gobby:` prefix naming convention (e.g., `gobby:pipeline-heartbeat`) to distinguish them from user-created jobs.

Phase 2 of gobby-cron scheduling refactor: Pipeline heartbeat monitoring transitions from separate daemon loop to registered cron handler. Implement as PipelineHeartbeat callable class, register at daemon startup. Auto-create `gobby:pipeline-heartbeat` system job on daemon init.

Database bloat pattern: Expired session deletions fragment ~/.gobby/gobby-hub.db causing significant file size growth (e.g., 7.5x bloat ratio, 2.8GB→400-600MB after VACUUM). Use VACUUM operation to reclaim space when bloat is detected.

Safe VACUUM procedure for gobby: use `gobby stop; sqlite3 ~/.gobby/gobby-hub.db "VACUUM;"; gobby start` executed offline (outside agent session) to avoid daemon write conflicts during exclusive lock. ~2.8 GB temporary free space required.
Session_messages table exhibits bloat pattern: 367 MB of content occupied 2,760 MB on disk (7.5x bloat ratio). Watch for recurring bloat in this table during database optimization work.

Gobby database location: ~/.gobby/gobby-hub.db. VACUUM command requires daemon to be stopped (exclusive lock needed). Syntax: sqlite3 ~/.gobby/gobby-hub.db "VACUUM;". Needs ~2x database size in free disk space.
Task #9935 (feature request) determined out of scope for Gobby—refers to Claude Code's TaskOutput API. Prevents future re-investigation of this item.

`show_file` MCP tool architecture: backend spans canvas.py, broadcast.py (broadcast_artifact_event function), and http.py wiring; frontend uses artifact_event handler in useChat.ts and createArtifact integration in ChatPage.tsx. Supports multiple media types (code, markdown, base64 images, CSV) with automatic detection via file extension. 11 passing tests validate multi-format support.
Task #9935 is out of scope for gobby — it refers to Claude Code's TaskOutput, not gobby's internal structures. If encountered again, reject or redirect without investigation.
Merge agent verification steps and merge-related testing were removed from testing system (Task #9972). Testing guide no longer covers merge agent validation—avoid looking for it in future test work.

Testing guide restructured into 8 sections with component-level isolation: Section 2 (Expand-Task standalone with wait_for_completion), Section 3 (Dev-Loop with manual worktree + continuation_prompt), Section 4 (Full Orchestrator integration), Section 5 (Regression with 8 smoke tests). Expand-task and dev-loop can run independently without full orchestrator.
Testing skill (v2.0.0) includes component selection prompt that gates test scope upfront to: expand only, dev-loop only, integration, regression, or all tests

Heartbeat mechanism in orchestration context is architecturally distinct from timeout handling—heartbeats provide periodic liveness signals for detecting alive-but-unresponsive processes, while timeouts wait for result delivery

Orchestration system identified four potential failure modes for heartbeat mechanism: (1) stalled executors hanging without timing out, (2) zombie agents losing parent connection but continuing to run, (3) conductor unable to distinguish 'slow but working' from 'dead' without liveness signals, (4) test flakiness from lack of component readiness confirmation

Pre-existing test failure in bundled content sync tests: `assert 21 == 19` occurs when bundled variables are added without updating the test count assertion. Pattern: when adding bundled variables to bundled content, sync test assertions that verify expected counts must be synchronized.
test_bundled_variables_sync_to_db test has incorrect variable count expectation. Tracked as issue #9919, not yet fixed. This test will need expectation adjustment when variables sync is modified.

Merge agent removal was intentional pre-testing cleanup. Agent code deleted in commits c0508c2c and 83aa4ae6; pipeline step removal already completed in c0508c2c. Pipeline configurations and agent code remained in sync—no additional cleanup needed.
Bundled variables count is 21 (from gobby-default-variables.yaml), validated by test_bundled_variables_sync_to_db. Was incorrectly documented as 19 until corrected in commit 5ec7c21b.

Artifacts panel is fully implemented at web/src/components/chat/artifacts/ with all major viewers: code viewer, text viewer, image viewer, spreadsheet viewer, plus resize handle and version history. Feature is complete.
TaskOutput class and retrieval_status attribute are external to gobby codebase - they belong to Claude Code's built-in tool infrastructure. Do not search gobby source for these features.

Bundled template variable counts in YAML must stay synchronized with test assertions. When templates are updated with new variable definitions, the corresponding test assertion checking variable count must be updated to match. Variable count mismatches cause test failures — #9947 failure was due to YAML having 21 variables while test assertion expected 19. Check bundled template YAML variable definitions against test assertions when debugging related test failures.

8 tasks remain open with specific blockers: #9935 (TaskOutput retrieval_status), #9943 (test assertion mismatch), #9945 (artifacts panel feature), #9946 & #9953 (cross-machine sync exploration), #9947 (test count mismatch), #9955 (gobby pack feature), #9968 (stale edit-write-recovery state)

tmux intercepts Shift+Enter by default, preventing multi-line input in Claude Code terminal. Root cause: tmux treats Shift+Enter as plain Enter instead of transmitting kitty-protocol escape sequence \x1b[13;2u. Fix: add `bind-key -n S-Enter send-keys Escape "[13;2u"` to ~/.tmux.conf, then reload with `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf`. Terminal must support kitty-protocol.

Antigravity's terminal emulator is xterm.js-based and treats Shift+Enter identically to regular Enter at the escape sequence level. This prevents tmux from distinguishing between the two inputs and is an environmental limitation, not a keybinding/configuration issue. Any Shift+Enter keybinding workarounds will fail in Antigravity's terminal specifically.

Antigravity's built-in terminal (xterm.js) sends Shift+Enter as a bare carriage return, indistinguishable from regular Enter. This is a terminal emulator limitation preventing differentiation at the tmux/application layer—not a tmux or Claude Code defect.

Josh's dev environment: Antigravity (VS Code fork) built-in terminal + tmux. Shift+Enter fix: Antigravity keybindings.json at ~/Library/Application Support/Antigravity/User/keybindings.json has a sendSequence binding for shift+enter — must send \u001b[13;2u (CSI u) not ' \n'. Also has ~/.claude/keybindings.json with shift+enter -> chat:newline. tmux has extended-keys on + xterm*:extkeys.

To work around Antigravity terminal keybinding issues, check VS Code settings: `terminal.integrated.sendKeybindingsToShell` (needs enabling) and `terminal.integrated.commandsToSkipShell` (may intercept keys before reaching terminal).

tmux S-Enter keybindings can intercept shift+enter keystrokes before Claude Code receives them, preventing chat:newline action. If shift+enter malfunction in Claude Code, check tmux config and remove conflicting S-Enter bindings.

Claude Code uses `~/.claude/keybindings.json` for keybinding customization. Created file with mapping: `shift+enter` → `chat:newline`. Requires Claude Code restart to reload keybindings after file creation or modification.

Tmux S-Enter keybindings can interfere with Claude Code input handling; conflicts must be removed from tmux config and explicitly configured in ~/.claude/keybindings.json, followed by tmux reload and Claude Code restart for changes to take effect
~/.claude/keybindings.json can explicitly bind shift+enter to 'chat:newline' action to override tmux interference and prevent unwanted space character insertion during Claude Code chat input
tmux S-Enter keybind intercepts and corrupts Claude Code's shift+enter escape sequences, causing space character insertion; remove from tmux config and use ~/.claude/keybindings.json instead
Claude Code shift+enter keybinding for chat:newline requires explicit configuration in ~/.claude/keybindings.json (command lacks default binding)

Shift+Enter in tmux requires TWO steps: (1) extended-keys support enabled (extended-keys on; xterm*:extkeys), AND (2) explicit Shift+Enter passthrough bind in ~/.tmux.conf. Extended-keys alone is insufficient; the explicit bind is the missing piece when Shift+Enter doesn't produce newline.

Gobby's `require-error-triage` pre-commit hook can be satisfied by confirming that only non-code files (e.g., markdown) were modified during a session; lint and test execution steps can be skipped when no code changes are present. The hook enforces this check during stop/commit operations.
To clear the [require-memory-review] gate blocking session stop in Gobby, call set_variable(name='pending_memory_review', value=false). This gate must be cleared before session termination.

Gobby's edit-write-recovery hook rule blocks the stop command when an Edit/Write operation fails. The hook must be cleared by retrying or completing the failed file write operation before stop is permitted.

Merge agent removed from autonomous dev-loop and orchestrator pipelines (commit c0508c2c). Merge is now manual — the pipeline terminates when all tasks reach review_approved status. The merge agent definition still exists but is not spawned by any pipeline. Reason: pushing to remote and closing tasks autonomously is the highest-risk operation and should be tested on a test project with packaged gobby before enabling. The merge agent also has a no-push conflict with worker-safety rules that needs resolution (push should be an MCP tool, not raw Bash).

Two implementation approaches proposed: (1) targeted - remove tag:worker-safety from merge agent's rule_selectors, (2) broader - remove entire workflows block and revert to step enforcement only (pending user choice)
User decided to remove merge from the dev/qa loop and defer the broader 'lockdown' mechanism for enforcing workflow state transitions
Merge agent configuration at src/gobby/agents/merge/ currently has a workflows block with tag:worker-safety filtering that enforces dev/qa loop participation

Merge workflow security gap: merge agent can push to arbitrary branches via `git push origin HEAD:some-other-branch` because it retains Bash access with no push validation. Current rules (`no-force-push`, `no-destructive-git`) and `blocked_tools: [Write, Edit]` don't constrain target branch.

Two `no-push` rules serve different purposes: (1) `no-push` blocks ALL spawned agents via `is_spawned_agent` variable check, universal scope; (2) `no-push-for-workers` restricts to `["developer", "qa-reviewer"]` agents only via explicit `agent_scope`. Merge agent needs exclusion from first rule only, second rule naturally scopes to workers.

Rule engine applies `_active_rule_names` filtering before evaluating `when` conditions. `agent_scope: null` functions as universal scope. Explicit `agent_scope` arrays restrict rule application to named agent types only. This distinction is critical: `is_spawned_agent` variable acts as primary blocker regardless of scope, while `agent_scope` provides secondary type-based scoping.

Merge agent requires explicit `name:no-push` exclusion in its rule configuration to enable push operations. The `no-push` rule blocks ALL spawned agents via `variables.get('is_spawned_agent')` check regardless of `agent_scope`. Without explicit exclusion, merge agent inherits `no-push` via `tag:worker-safety` match despite being a separate spawned agent type.

Merge agent blocked by tag:worker-safety push rules (no-push, no-push-for-workers)? Use exclude: ["tag:sync", "name:no-push", "name:no-push-for-workers"] in agent config to allow pushes while keeping other safety protections like no-destructive-git and no-force-push.

install/shared/ directory reorganized (task #9960): workflow definitions moved to install/shared/workflows/ subdirectories: agents/, rules/, variables/, and pipelines/. Deprecated/ subdirectories (previously in each workflow type) were removed.

JSONL sync architecture: All task/memory sync rules are now in workflows/rules/sync/ tagged with 'sync'. Agent definitions use rule_selectors with exclude: ['tag:sync'] to prevent JSONL contamination in worktrees. Git hooks (pre-commit, post-commit, post-merge, post-checkout) also skip sync when GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID env var is set. The sync/ group has 6 rules: task-sync-import-on-start, task-sync-export-on-end, task-sync-export-on-compact, memory-sync-import, memory-sync-export-on-end, memory-sync-export-on-compact.

Template files restructured: agents, rules, variables, and pipelines all live under src/gobby/install/shared/workflows/ now (not directly under shared/). Agent definitions are at workflows/agents/, rules at workflows/rules/, pipelines at workflows/pipelines/. The pre-commit hook triggered this rename during the #9958 commit. Deprecated directories were also cleaned up in that commit.

Task sync recovery procedure when local/remote diverge: (1) cp .gobby/tasks.jsonl .gobby/tasks.jsonl.backup, (2) fetch remote DB copy, (3) clear local .gobby/tasks.jsonl, (4) resync from remote, (5) cherry-pick new local tasks from backup using jq. Requires code fix (scoping memory gates to interactive sessions) to deploy first.

JSONL task import causes seq_num renumbering. During this session's import, 356 tasks were renumbered (e.g., remote #9734 became local #9557). Issue #9914 tracks preserving seq_nums on JSONL import. Root cause: import logic doesn't respect original seq_num values.

Adding `tag:default` scope to triage and memory gates rules is NOT an effective fix for gate rule evaluation issues (attempted for #9914, failed). Real solution requires properly implementing the dual-context split for discovery tool handling.

`is_discovery_tool` function (src/gobby/workflows/enforcement/blocking.py:46) is used in two distinct contexts: blocking enforcement and triage/gates rule evaluation. These have different requirements and may need separate tool set definitions or implementations.

Rules are being consolidated from separate files into combined files for release prep. `sync_bundled_rules` in the installers already has orphan cleanup logic (lines 395-420) that removes DB entries for rules whose bundled files no longer exist.

Architectural pattern: `sync_export` effects are migrating from `context-handoff` rules (tag:default) to dedicated `sync-*` files (tag:sync). This separation allows preservation of non-sync effects (like `extract_from_session`) in their original context-handoff files while centralizing sync operations.

Proposed consolidation plan for rules system: Delete `context-handoff/task-sync-import-on-start.yaml` and `memory-lifecycle/memory-sync-import.yaml`. Modify `context-handoff/preserve-context-on-end.yaml` and `preserve-context-on-compact.yaml` to remove only `sync_export` effects while retaining `extract_from_session` and variable resets. Create `sync-tasks.yaml` and `sync-memory.yaml` to consolidate task-sync and memory-sync imports. Net file count impact: zero.

Rule consolidation (adding tag:sync) and rule selector configuration (adding exclude directives) are tightly coupled - exclude directives only function after tags exist; should not be implemented as independent tasks
Sync rules consolidation uses tag:sync in sync-tasks.yaml and sync-memory.yaml; developer/QA/merge agents use rule_selectors with exclude: [tag:sync] to opt-out of sync rules
Pre-commit hook should skip JSONL sync when GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID env var is set; spawn_executor exports this variable to prevent feedback loops during orchestration context

Proposed sync-tasks.yaml consolidation: combine task-sync-import-on-start (session_start event), task-sync-export-on-end (session_end event), task-sync-export-on-compact (pre_compact event), all with tags [sync, lifecycle, gobby, default].

YAML rule consolidation pattern: a single YAML file can contain multiple rules under a `rules:` key with tags applied at the file level to all contained rules. Pattern previously used in memory-lifecycle.yaml and context-handoff.yaml (each containing 5+ rules).

For developer agent sync rule exclusion, prefer tag-based filtering ('tag:sync') over explicit name patterns. Any future sync rules tagged 'sync' are automatically excluded without configuration changes, providing better scalability.

Developer agents require exclusion of both export AND import sync rules. Import rules alone (task-sync-import-on-start, memory-sync-import) allow stale JSONL from worktree to re-import old task/memory state on session start, overwriting current database data. Must exclude: sync-export-on-end, sync-export-on-compact, task-sync-import-on-start, memory-sync-import.

GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID environment variable guard pattern: Export GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID from spawn_executor() and modify pre-commit hook to conditionally skip JSONL sync when this variable is set. Defense-in-depth approach to prevent accidental syncs during agent-managed executions.

Developer agent rule_selectors configuration pattern: include=["tag:default", "tag:worker-safety"] with exclude=["name:sync-export-on-end", "name:sync-export-on-compact", "name:task-sync-import-on-start", "name:memory-sync-import"]. Must exclude both export AND import sync rules to prevent stale JSONL re-imports and worktree export conflicts.

Rule splitting strategy for preserve rules: Split preserve-context-on-end and preserve-context-on-compact into separate rules — memory extraction rules (extract-memories-on-end, preserve-context-on-compact variant) tagged default, and sync rules (sync-export-on-end, sync-export-on-compact) tagged default. This allows rule_selectors to selectively exclude sync operations via name pattern while retaining memory extraction.

Sync rules must be excluded from developer agent `rule_selectors` because spawned agents execute in worktrees with no JSONL sync capability. This is an architectural constraint, not misconfiguration—sync rule conflicts are expected and required exclusions.

Stop gate enforcement for developer agents is hardcoded in the engine via tool_block_pending/stop_attempts auto-management in evaluate(), NOT via declarative rules in resolve_rules_for_agent(). Do not look for gate rules—they don't exist for developers.

Proposed JSONL sync contamination fix uses is_spawned_agent flag (set during SESSION_START) with guard `when: "not vars.is_spawned_agent"` on all four sync rules (preserve-context-on-end, preserve-context-on-compact, task-sync-import-on-start, memory-sync-import). Dual-vector solution: rules engine guard + separate GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID env var check in pre-commit hook (pattern exists: GOBBY_MACHINE_ID in spawn_executor.py).

JSONL sync contamination in spawned agents via four default-tagged rules: preserve-context-on-end (session_end trigger, exports memories.jsonl+tasks.jsonl), preserve-context-on-compact (pre_compact trigger, same exports), task-sync-import-on-start (session_start trigger, imports tasks.jsonl), memory-sync-import (session_start trigger, imports memories.jsonl). All fire for spawned agents causing bidirectional contamination (stale re-imports + worktree exports creating merge conflicts).

Solution pattern for pre-commit hook agent detection: Set GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID env var in spawn_executor.py (following existing GOBBY_MACHINE_ID pattern via `env["GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID"] = request.run_id`), then check `[ -n "$GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID" ]` in .git/hooks/pre-commit to skip sync exports during agent runs. Apply same pattern to memory sync hooks if present. This prevents JSONL exports in worktrees; syncs deferred to orchestrator/main.

Pre-commit hook conflict root cause: `gobby tasks sync --export --auto` in .git/hooks/pre-commit exports tasks.jsonl and memories.jsonl to any working directory (including worktrees/clones) without detecting agent execution context, creating guaranteed merge conflicts when orchestrator syncs from main. The hook has no mechanism to distinguish orchestrator commits (main) from agent commits (worktree).

Merge conflict prevention: When merging worktree branches back to main, stale JSONL files in the branch overwrite current main versions. Solution before merge: `git checkout main -- .gobby/tasks.jsonl .gobby/memories.jsonl` or configure .gitattributes with `ours` merge strategy for those files.

JSONL files (.gobby/tasks.jsonl, .gobby/memories.jsonl) are sync/export artifacts, not creation sources. SQLite daemon DB is source of truth. tasks.jsonl is exported via pre-commit hook: `gobby tasks sync --export --auto`. These files in worktrees are stale git-tracked copies.
dispatch_batch accepts agent parameter (defaults to 'developer'), making it reusable with any agent definition. Located in pipeline as configurable argument, not hardcoded.

INFRASTRUCTURE_TOOLS workaround: Allows agents to call set_variable to clear stop gates and bypass the gate restriction. Acknowledged as escape hatch, not proper solution. Use when gate architecture needs redesign.

Developer agent gate blocker: Calling mark_task_needs_review → on_mcp_success fires automatic workflow step transition from 'implement' to 'terminate' → stop gates (require-error-triage, require-memory-review) activate on next tool call → agent restricted to terminate step's allowed_tools (discovery tools only) → cannot perform work required by gates (lint, tests, task creation). Architectural conflict diagnosed 2026-03-07, requires pre-gate condition redesign.

Infrastructure tools exemption in src/gobby/enforcement/blocking.py: set_variable and get_variable are special-cased via INFRASTRUCTURE_TOOLS set and is_infrastructure_tool() function. These tools receive exemption from step enforcement rules. Pattern applies to both blocking.py and rule_engine.py (bug_9665, commit 15d57369).

Tool block clearing in rule_engine.py (~lines 220 and 250): ANY successful AFTER_TOOL event now unconditionally clears tool_block_pending, regardless of which tool succeeded. Previously required the exact blocked tool to succeed. Changed in both early-return and main evaluation paths (commit 15d57369).

set_variable and get_variable are classified as INFRASTRUCTURE_TOOLS and are exempt from step enforcement restrictions. Exemption implemented via is_infrastructure_tool() function checked at rule_engine.py:834. These tools bypass normal workflow step restrictions by design.

Task validation failures occur when task descriptions don't match actual implementation, particularly for 'code-index rule templates' and 'progressive discovery enforcement' tasks. These get routed to review (needs_review status) pending task description audit rather than closing directly.
INFRASTRUCTURE_TOOLS set in rule_engine.py includes set_variable and related utility functions. Controls how infrastructure tools are handled during rule evaluation.

In rule_engine.py, tool_block_pending clearing behavior changed: previously required the same blocked tool to succeed before clearing block state; now unconditionally clears on ANY successful AFTER_TOOL execution. Changes applied to both early-return path (~line 220) and main evaluation path (~line 250).

Task status transitions are enforced: you cannot use `update_task` to change a closed/blocked task back to open. Must use `reopen_task` tool instead. `update_task` will silently fail or error on status changes that require specific transition tools.

The reopen_task tool successfully transitions tasks from review_approved status back to open status. This allows batch reopening of review_approved tasks without requiring alternative workflows like approve_and_cleanup.

Task sync import code in src/gobby/sync/tasks.py has a known issue: when handling seq_num collisions, it assigns fresh numbers to imported items, which breaks existing references. This must be fixed (#9914) before implementing session sync (#9915) or pack functionality (#9916).

Task sync produces unexplained +3 task count discrepancy: JSONL has 6426 tasks total, but after importing 356 new tasks to DB (6073→6429), the math shows 3 extra tasks were created during import that don't exist in source file. Root cause unknown—investigate to prevent data drift in syncs.

JSONL-to-DB sync for tasks can have discrepancies: DB may end up with more records than JSONL (6429 vs 6426) due to tasks created during sync or seq_num collisions. Memory sync deduplicates by content, so different IDs with same content are expected (57 found in one sync). Task #9913 tracks exploring better cross-device sync.

commit 68843861 assigns fresh seq_nums during JSONL import via DB auto-increment, not preserving values from file. SQLite's sqlite_sequence table provides new numbers based on highest ever used, causing seq_num divergence across machines while UUIDs remain stable
seq_num preservation on JSONL import is technically feasible with no identified barriers; collision handling would use conditional logic (preserve if available, assign fresh only on collision)

JSONL import (commit 68843861) deliberately regenerates seq_nums on import rather than preserving values from the file. This is intentional design for cross-machine portability—the same data imported on different machines receives different #N shorthand references. seq_num values are not stored in JSONL format.

JSONL task import fails with unique constraint violation on (project_id, seq_num) because INSERT statements for new tasks include explicit seq_num values from JSONL that collide with existing project sequences. Fix: omit seq_num from INSERT for new tasks and let database auto-assign, since seq_num is auto-generated and project-local.

seq_num collision in import_from_jsonl() blocks multi-record task/memory imports. Duplicate seq_num assignments prevent imports from completing. Fixed in commit 68843861. Function location: likely src/gobby/storage/migrations.py or related import code.

Adding `tag:default` to triage and memory gate rules is NOT the correct fix for gate-related routing bugs. The gates use tag-based scoping but the real issue is the `is_discovery_tool` function being shared across gate enforcement and triage routing contexts.

`is_discovery_tool` in `src/gobby/workflows/enforcement/blocking.py` is used in two distinct contexts: (1) gate enforcement (blocking.py) where it exempts read-only tools from stop gates, and (2) triage routing where it identifies tools that gather info vs. perform actions. These are semantically different needs that were conflated into one function/set, causing bugs when the triage gate fired on discovery tools. Fix requires splitting into separate functions with separate tool sets.

Task seq_num collision detection in JSONL import (src/gobby/sync/tasks.py): preserves all existing seq_nums from remote source; fresh seq_nums only assigned to local-only tasks lacking seq_nums. This ensures no duplicate seq_nums across entire corpus when merging remote+local tasks.

Task JSONL import seq_num recovery (March 2026): After the seq_num preservation fix in src/gobby/sync/tasks.py, a full data recovery was performed. Cleared all 6437 tasks from DB, restored remote JSONL (6426 tasks with correct seq_nums), appended 11 local-only tasks without seq_nums, and reimported. The 11 local tasks got fresh seq_nums 9946-9956. The fix works by building occupied_seq_nums and batch_claimed tracking dicts before import, then preserving JSONL seq_nums when available and unoccupied, falling back to MAX+1 only on collision.

Adding tag:default to triage/memory gate scope rules is an incorrect remediation for agent gate enforcement issues. Gate rules are correct as-is; misdiagnosis caused by confusion between gate rule logic and step enforcement blocking.

Issue #9665: Agent gate enforcement failure root cause is NOT gate rules themselves, but step enforcement blocking set_variable/get_variable. Correct fix is to exempt these variable tools from step enforcement via INFRASTRUCTURE_TOOLS mechanism, NOT by adding tag:default to gate scope rules.
Gates should fire for agents; fix is granting agents access to `set_variable` rather than modifying gate logic itself. Avoid changing gate firing conditions when the issue is agent permissions.

Test failure #9919 (variable count assertion in rule engine tests) is pre-existing and remains open. Filed during task #9918 closure with commit 0a94bd8f. All 68 rule engine tests pass except this one assertion.

When gate enforcement appears to fail for agents, investigate access control first. Gates exist to enforce compliance rules and should not be disabled as a workaround. The distinction: gate YAML rules define what should happen; function permissions define what agents can do. These are separate concerns.

Agents currently lack permission to execute `set_variable` function calls. This is an access control issue that must be fixed in system permissions/function definitions (not by modifying gate YAML configuration). Root cause of task #9918 failure; distinguishing factor from gate enforcement rules.

When .gobby/tasks.jsonl diverges between local and remote (e.g., seq_nums renumbered during import), recovery procedure: 1) cp tasks.jsonl tasks_backup.jsonl, 2) grab remote version, 3) clear out tasks, 4) resync, 5) cherry-pick new local-only tasks from backup. The import bug can renumber hundreds of tasks (356 observed in one case).
set_variable and get_variable are top-level MCP tools with mcp__gobby__ namespace (mcp__gobby__set_variable, mcp__gobby__get_variable), not wrapped by call_tool
Step enforcement logic in rule_engine.py lines 834-837 applies allowed_tools restrictions only to non-discovery tools by checking is_discovery_tool() before blocking
EXEMPT_TOOLS in constants.py is dead code and unused; the actual exemption mechanism is DISCOVERY_TOOLS in enforcement/blocking.py, checked via is_discovery_tool() function

Stop gates `require-memory-review` and `require-error-triage` cause agents to stall for 3+ minutes at prompts when firing (agents can't satisfy interactive concerns). Solution: add `agent_scope: ["default"]` to both rule definitions in rule engine config to filter them out via `_filter_by_agent_scope` infrastructure.

When agents stall at stop prompts for 3+ minutes, check if interactive stop gate rules (requiring human review/decision) have `agent_scope` filtering applied. Add `agent_scope: ["default"]` to gates agents cannot satisfy interactively.

Stop gate rules `require-memory-review` and `require-error-triage` must use `agent_scope: ["default"]` configuration to exclude them from agent sessions. Agents cannot interactively satisfy these gates and idled for 3+ minutes at prompt without filtering. Solution leverages existing `_filter_by_agent_scope` infrastructure in rule engine.

agent_scope filtering pattern: Apply scope constraints to stop gate rules requiring interactive/manual input agents cannot perform. Constraint value "default" excludes rules from all agent execution contexts (dev, qa, merge, agent, etc.). Enables environment-specific rule behavior without rule duplication.

Stop gate rules require-memory-review and require-error-triage cause agent sessions to hang because agents cannot perform manual review/triage. Fix: add agent_scope: ["default"] constraint to both rules to exclude them from agent execution contexts. Uses existing _filter_by_agent_scope infrastructure in rule engine.

Ruff formatter cannot format YAML files - it will fail/skip YAML files when attempting to format. This is a known limitation of ruff (Python-focused formatter). If YAML files are in staged changes and ruff is run as part of pre-commit hooks, formatting errors will occur.
New rules in YAML configuration require `agent_scope: ['default']` setting to pass validation during rule engine implementation.

#9665 fix approach: Replace reliance on _agent_type with is_spawned_agent guard in stop-gate when clauses. is_spawned_agent is reliably set during SESSION_START (line 864) via session.agent_run_id check, making it a safer guard than _agent_type which has timing issues.

#9665 root cause: _agent_type set in initial_variables but doesn't persist before SESSION_START fires, causing _resolve_agent_name to default to 'default' and incorrectly load all tag:default rules including stop gates. This is a variable persistence timing issue.
Memories use a backup-only pattern (not live sync), with startup import based on entry count and content-based dedup. No CLI import command exists for memories, unlike tasks.

Sessions storage is SQLite-only with no sync infrastructure implemented yet. It maintains an independent seq_num and path_cache system separate from task sync, making it a greenfield implementation target.
Current task/memory sync mechanism is fragile due to seq_num collision issues. Created task #9913 to design better sync strategy. Fragility is architectural—needs redesign rather than patch fixes.

Memories table deduplicates by content hash, not by ID. Session found 57 memories with IDs missing from DB but 100% were content duplicates (zero truly missing data). Important for import/sync logic handling ID collisions—won't lose unique content even if IDs don't match.

close_task tool has undocumented behavior: when a task contains edits to files outside the repo (e.g., ~/.tmux.conf), it automatically routes the task to 'review' status instead of closing it. This prevents out-of-repo changes from being discarded and provides a safety mechanism for non-code edits.

Gobby file edit operations require task association enforced by hook_dispatcher (~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py). Personal/non-project files cannot be edited through Gobby tools—the 'tool-failure-recovery' rule blocks edits without task context. Manual edits bypass this constraint.

To apply server option changes in tmux without disruptive `tmux kill-server`: create a new session (`tmux new-session -d -s fresh`), switch to it, then kill the old session. The new session implicitly reads server config at creation time, picking up server-level changes like `extended-keys`.

tmux server options (e.g., `set -s extended-keys`) only take effect for new sessions/windows created AFTER the server reads the config. Running `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf` reloads the config but does NOT apply server options to existing sessions—a misconception that leads to confusion.

The gobby daemon must be running to execute stop hooks (hook_dispatcher.py). If the daemon is not running and you attempt a stop operation, you'll get an error. Solution: run `gobby start` first to bring the daemon online before executing stop operations.

Shift+Enter keybinding in tmux not working (printing escape sequences): Use `set -as terminal-features 'xterm*:extkeys'` in ~/.tmux.conf. Remove conflicting deprecated `xterm-keys on` directive if present. Requires full tmux server restart (but can use new-session workaround instead of disruptive `tmux kill-server`; config reload alone is insufficient).

Tmux extended-keys configuration: Use modern `terminal-features 'xterm*:extkeys'` instead of deprecated `terminal-overrides` or `xterm-keys on`. The `extended-keys` option is server-level and requires a server restart to take effect—but instead of disruptive `tmux kill-server`, create a new session (`tmux new-session -d -s fresh`), switch to it, and kill the old session.

tmux extended-keys config can regress after machine restart or terminal emulator updates—it doesn't persist without being in ~/.tmux.conf. Terminal emulators emit Shift+Enter as different escape sequences (CSI u, eOM, etc.), and tmux needs extended-keys enabled to recognize them.

Shift+Enter in Claude Code editor (tmux) requires extended-keys mode. Add to ~/.tmux.conf: 'set -s extended-keys on' and 'set -as terminal-features "xterm*:extkeys"'. Reload with 'tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf'

Agents only use TaskOutput because Claude Code's run_in_background: true requires it. Synchronous orchestrator model doesn't need background execution. Long-running commands signal lifecycle monitor problems, not legitimate async needs.

TaskOutput (Claude Code's built-in CLI tool) has design flaw: returns retrieval_status: success even when underlying Bash command fails. Decision made to block TaskOutput via PreToolUse hook rule to enforce synchronous execution. If background commands needed in future, build through Gobby infrastructure, not Claude Code.

Commit 91644c87 in normalization layer already detects Bash failures (exit_code != 0). Future sessions debugging command failures should reference this existing detection mechanism before implementing workarounds.

Gobby's normalization layer (AFTER_TOOL hooks) already detects Bash failures. Commit 91644c87 demonstrates the pattern. Potential mitigation for TaskOutput ambiguity: detect `retrieval_status: success` AND `exit_code != 0`, then inject clarifying context to LLM.

TaskOutput field `retrieval_status: success` means 'I fetched the command output OK', NOT 'the command succeeded'. This dual semantic meaning causes confusion about operation success. Actual command success is indicated by `exit_code`.

TaskOutput is a Claude Code built-in tool, baked into Claude Code's CLI and outside Gobby's control. Any modifications to its response format or fields require changes upstream in Claude Code, not in Gobby.

SQLite's sqlite_sequence metadata table persists across task deletions and tracks the highest seq_num ever used. Manual counter reset is impossible—re-imported tasks after deletion will receive new seq_nums starting from the previous maximum value, not from 1.

TaskOutput.retrieval_status field does not exist in codebase. Pipeline exit detection was addressed separately by issue #9789, making this field unnecessary. Future searches for this field should reference #9789 instead.

cleanup_stale_pending_runs() function exists in codebase but is NOT called during startup initialization—it must be invoked explicitly via CLI command. This was identified investigating issue #9841 (agents stuck in pending after restart). Function location should be verified in next session.

Child tasks don't automatically transition to closed status when implemented under a parent ticket in a single commit. This creates dangling references where completed work isn't reflected in task tracking. Example: tasks #9737, #9697, #9721 were fixed by commit 6a34cad5 under parent #9937 but remained open/in_progress status and required manual closure. When investigating multi-task closures, check git history for the parent ticket.

To wipe and reinstall skills/workflows from templates: DELETE FROM workflow_definitions + DELETE FROM skills, then call sync_bundled_content_to_db() followed by install_all_templates(). As of last run: 225 workflow_definitions and 61 skills were wiped, sync restored 45 templates + 61 skills, install created 180 installed workflows.

Gobby's task import uses timestamp-based comparison for deduplication (unlike memory import which uses content matching). Records are skipped when DB timestamps are newer than JSONL source timestamps, creating a different conflict resolution strategy between task and memory imports.

Gobby uses content-based deduplication in memory import via `content_exists` check at line 281 in import logic. Records are matched by exact content, not ID, which prevents re-importing identical records from JSONL sources even if the source file is updated. File path not fully specified but deduplication method is `content_exists`.

uv tool install . required for global CLI registration (different from pip install -e). Necessary in managed Python environments for gobby command to be available globally. Standard pip install -e does not properly register the CLI.

gobby CLI tool is only installed to .venv/bin/ (via [project.scripts] entry point), breaking bare 'gobby' invocations outside venv activation. Workaround: 'uv run gobby' works from project directory. Four approaches to resolve permanently: (1) global install via uv tool/pipx, (2) explicit path to .venv/bin/gobby, (3) use 'uv run' wrapper in rules, (4) runtime path resolution in rule engine. Choice depends on intended installation strategy (unresolved design question).
Canonical gobby installation path is undetermined; must be clarified before designing cross-project rules (candidates: ~/.gobby/bin/gobby, pipx managed, system PATH)
Cross-project gobby invocation cannot rely on project-local uv or pyproject.toml; must resolve to absolute path or use dynamic path resolution at runtime

Dispatcher hooks invoke `gobby` directly instead of using `uv run python`, conflicting with Bash pre-tool-use enforcement. Solution: reinstall hooks with `uv run python -m gobby.cli install` to regenerate dispatcher paths with correct invocation syntax (uv wrapper required).

When diagnosing hook failures, hook infrastructure issues are independent of session-level systems (e.g., memory review gates). Hook problems are typically root-caused to invocation convention mismatches or path regeneration needs.

Claude Code shell initialization fails with 'gobby: command not found' when shell config files (.zshrc, .zprofile, .zshenv) contain `eval "$(gobby ...)"` and the gobby binary is not in PATH during profile execution. Root causes: gobby binary missing from PATH in Claude Code's shell context, or Python venv not activated before profile initialization. This is a separate issue from database/migration problems and should be diagnosed by checking shell config files for eval statements.

seq_num collision bug exists in task sync pipeline during JSONL→DB sync; causes sequence number conflicts that prevent task state transitions and trigger stop hook block on task closure. Located in task sync logic. Identified and fixed during Exchange 8. If similar 'command not found: gobby' errors with stop hook enforcement occur, check task sync seq_num validation.

Claude Code's subprocess environment (used by hook dispatcher) may not inherit PATH from shell initialization files (.bashrc/.zshrc), causing 'gobby command not found' errors even when 'uv sync --reinstall-package gobby' succeeds and gobby works in direct terminal. Diagnose with: 'which gobby' and 'gobby status' in direct terminal vs hook dispatcher context.

Venv corruption in this project can be isolated to entry point installation issues vs full dependency tree issues. When diagnosing CLI failures, check if entry points are stale before assuming full dependency rebuild is needed.

When gobby CLI entry point is missing/inaccessible (causing hook dispatcher to fail), run `uv sync --reinstall-package gobby` to rebuild entry points without full venv recreation. This is faster than deleting/recreating the venv from scratch.

Pre-commit hooks and venv have circular dependency: hook dispatcher needs `gobby` CLI entry point on PATH (provided by initialized venv), but `uv sync` may fail due to hook interference. When encountering 'command not found: gobby' error or pre-commit failures during environment setup, run `uv sync` first for clean venv reinstall before running tests or hooks. This resolves the entry point availability and breaks the circular dependency.

JSONL import logic naively includes auto-generated field values without conflict detection; similar seq_num-like collisions could occur with other auto-generated fields if included in JSONL payload. Pattern to watch: any auto-generated field in import must either be omitted or have explicit conflict handling (INSERT OR REPLACE, etc.)

When using `sync_bundled_content_to_db()`, only `template` source definitions are synced/reinstalled; project-scoped (`project` source) and user-installed (`installed` source) definitions are preserved and unaffected by the sync

`sync_bundled_content_to_db()` in `cli/installers/shared.py` is the function to use for complete template sync and auto-installation; it resyncs all bundled templates and automatically installs skill templates and gobby-tagged workflow definitions

Hub type validation in daemon startup lacks graceful degradation. Removing a hub type from the Literal union leaves existing DB entries with that type, causing daemon crash. No migration framework or schema versioning exists to handle hub type deprecation. Future hub type refactoring requires either DB cleanup or startup validation fallback.

config_store table can contain stale hub type entries causing daemon startup crash. Valid hub types are: clawdhub, skillsmp, github-collection, claude-plugins. If daemon fails during startup with validation error, check config_store for rows with invalid type values.

WakeDispatcher has two tmux sender paths: `tmux_sender` (for agent tmux sessions on -L gobby socket) and `tmux_pane_sender` (for invoking CLI panes on the default tmux socket). The pane sender uses `tmux send-keys -t <pane_id>` without -L flag. Pane IDs come from `terminal_context.tmux_pane` on the session record.

AgentLifecycleMonitor now accepts `completion_registry` parameter and fires `completion_registry.notify()` when it detects dead agents (tmux, autonomous, or orphaned DB runs). This is critical for orchestrator continuations — without it, agent death doesn't trigger pipeline re-invocation.

In spawner.py, call has_session() immediately after create_session() to verify tmux session creation succeeded—tmux can silently fail to create sessions, and without this post-spawn verification, dead/orphaned sessions propagate downstream causing orchestrator deadlocks.

lifecycle_monitor.py: completion_registry parameter wired to notify() for three scenarios—dead tmux agents, autonomous agents, orphaned DB runs—integrated with runner.py to fire completion notifications and unblock orchestrator continuations. This is the critical reliability pattern for preventing deadlocks.
SIGTERM signaling to orphaned PIDs must be added when lifecycle_monitor.py detects dead tmux sessions to properly clean up stranded processes and prevent resource leaks.

spawner.py: add has_session() verification immediately after create_session() to detect silent tmux failures. create_session() can fail without raising exceptions, requiring explicit post-spawn verification.

lifecycle_monitor.py: accepts completion_registry parameter and wires notify() calls to signal orchestration continuations when dead tmux sessions, autonomous agents, or orphaned DB runs are detected. This unblocks orchestrator when partial dispatch occurs.

MCP proxy has an argument unpacking bug: calls to tools like `claim_task` and `get_task` fail with 'create_lifecycle_registry' or 'create_crud_registry' errors, even with single arguments. Root cause is in the proxy's argument passing logic—arguments aren't being unpacked correctly before passing to functions. Affects multiple tool types across the proxy layer.
When `orchestration_complete: true`, `PipelineExecutor._notify_completion()` builds human-readable message and passes to `CompletionEventRegistry.notify(message=...)` to notify invoking session

Continuation prompt propagation requires three coordinated changes: (1) add `continuation_prompt` parameter to `_register_pipeline_continuation()` in `src/gobby/runner/_pipelines.py`, (2) add `_continuation_prompt` input with forwarding in `orchestrator.yaml` `register_continuations` step, (3) implement extraction/injection in `runner._rerun_pipeline()`
Auto-subscribe invoking session to continuation passes by calling `_auto_subscribe_lineage()` in `runner._rerun_pipeline()` post-execution (after pipeline runs, before returning)

Orchestration completion notifications: `PipelineExecutor._notify_completion()` generates human-readable completion messages when `orchestration_complete: true`, passes message to `CompletionEventRegistry.notify(message=...)` for invoking session notification

Continuation prompt threading pattern: parameters must be added to orchestrator.yaml's register_continuations step (e.g., _continuation_prompt input with forwarding), then extracted and injected in runner._rerun_pipeline() to propagate through continuation passes

Task status notification system implemented in _notifications.py::notify_parent_on_status_change() as fire-and-forget function; broadcasts task_progress events via CompletionEventRegistry.notify(message=...) at 6 status transition points across codebase

Auto-subscribe invoking session to continuation passes by calling _auto_subscribe_lineage() in runner._rerun_pipeline() after run_pipeline() returns; ensures parent session receives all downstream continuation pass updates

tmux session creation broken post-daemon-restart (#9897) — root blocker preventing agent execution. Test battery couldn't proceed past first agent spawn after daemon restart. Fix this before other agent lifecycle issues.

Orchestrator core logic verified working: task expansion, dependency resolution, QA approval all passed. Test failures traced to agent lifecycle/terminal layer, not orchestration design. Failures are daemon resilience gaps, not architectural flaws.

Daemon restart during orchestration leaves in-flight agents orphaned with no recovery pathway (#9893). Daemon lacks persistence of orchestration state across restarts. Missing continuation mechanism to wake CLI with pending work.
In src/gobby/workflows/rule_engine.py, tool_block_pending clearing checks _last_blocked_tool match before clearing—prevents unrelated parallel tool calls from neutralizing the stop gate
Cannot use skip_validation flag together with commit flag—stop hook tool-failure-recovery rule will block this combination. Retry validation without skip_validation instead

In src/gobby/workflows/rule_engine.py, the after_tool clearing logic for tool_block_pending must verify that the completing tool matches _last_blocked_tool before clearing the flag. This prevents unrelated parallel tool calls from neutralizing the stop gate in concurrent execution contexts.

In src/gobby/workflows/rule_engine.py, edit_write_pending lifecycle: set flag on BEFORE_TOOL for Edit/Write tools, cleared only on successful AFTER_TOOL completion. Includes circuit breaker with 3-attempt maximum before activating stop gate block with [edit-write-recovery] message to prevent infinite retry loops.

src/gobby/workflows/rule_engine.py: In after_tool clearing paths for tool_block_pending, validate completing tool matches _last_blocked_tool before clearing the gate. This prevents parallel tool calls from cross-contaminating stop gates.

Gobby orchestrator issue #9894: `continuation_prompt` mechanism doesn't wake CLI session via tmux—critical blocker preventing continuation prompt propagation (#9888) and completion notifications (#9889) from functioning. Root cause of orchestrator continuation pipeline infrastructure gap.

`_rerun_pipeline` is called by `CompletionEventRegistry` when agents complete; it invokes `run_pipeline` to re-run the orchestrator, and a subscription mechanism ensures the invoking session receives re-run notifications
#9897: tmux session creation broken after daemon restart—prevents headless agents from starting. Root cause of #9896. Blocks orchestrator test battery continuation until resolved.

Continuation metadata (continuation_prompt) must be propagated through three files when extending pipeline continuation: add parameter to `_pipelines.py::_register_pipeline_continuation()`, add `_continuation_prompt` input to `orchestrator.yaml` register_continuations step, and extract/inject in `runner._rerun_pipeline()`

Invoking sessions must be auto-subscribed to continuation pass notifications by calling `_auto_subscribe_lineage` post-execution in `runner._rerun_pipeline()` to ensure they receive all continuation events

Orchestrator state recovery pattern: Re-triggering with preserved worktree state and iteration count successfully restores async continuation chain, allowing iteration loop to resume. Validated in `pe-f895ae26` recovery sequence.

Merge spawn gate behavior: `spawn_merge()` skips execution when not all tasks have completed their signal, even if individual tasks are marked review_approved (e.g., #9886 stayed queued pending #9884 and #9885 completion). Merge requires ALL tasks → completion signal before triggering.

find_next() dependency filtering: Returns only ready tasks and correctly excludes blocked dependents per dependency graph. Example: #9884 returned for dispatch, #9885 excluded while #9884 pending. Validation confirms dependency enforcement in task sequencing.

rule_engine.py lines 217-221: tool_block_pending flag is cleared by ANY successful after_tool callback, not just the tool that set it. This causes premature flag clearing when parallel tools execute (e.g., one tool errors, its sibling gets cancelled and fires PostToolUse with is_failure=False).

Fix for tool_block_pending clearing logic: make it scope-aware by tracking which tool/tool-class originally set the flag, and only clear it upon that same tool's successful completion. Current naive logic violates assumption that flag state must correlate with originating context.

Claude SDK PostToolUse semantics unclear for cancelled parallel tool calls and is_failure flag interpretation in cancellation context. Recommended to file issue with Claude Code to clarify this behavior.

Orchestrator contains expansion as a native built-in step. The expand_epic step (ID 483) is marked skipped when expansion already performed separately, but in production workflow, running orchestrator once handles full sequence: expand → dispatch → QA → merge. Expansion and orchestration pipelines are distinct but coupled.

Test battery structure uses separate sections for expansion and orchestration (granular verification), but production workflow expects unified orchestrator pass. This structural difference is intentional and separation in test structure can be eliminated in user flow once #9884 lands.

Variable functions in `src/gobby/mcp_proxy/tools/workflows/_variables.py` (`set_variable()`, `get_variable()`, `set_session_variable()`) are tested in `tests/mcp_proxy/test_top_level_variables.py` (integration) and `tests/mcp_proxy/tools/workflows/test_variables.py` (unit tests)—saves hunting for test files when modifying these functions.

Variable function return values are not consumed by HTTP routes (server.py) or `__init__.py` re-exports, so changes to return key names are isolated to the variable functions themselves with minimal regression risk.

sessions.py:251 - transcript_manager requires lazy initialization pattern to avoid crashes when session_manager is None at initialization time. Use lazy loading rather than eager initialization in sessions router.

E2E workflow test assertions must be synchronized when tools are promoted from workflow-specific to top-level API. set_variable and get_variable were promoted, requiring removal from expected workflow tools lists in test files.

Variable setting implementation should use the workflows server's `set_session_variable` endpoint, not a top-level `set_variable` tool path. Initial approach using top-level tool was incorrect; workflows server integration is the proper implementation strategy.

gobby tools like `kill_agent`, `send_message` are blocked by step enforcement in PreToolUse hooks, preventing interactive agent control mid-session. Attempting these raises 'hook error' without further action possible.

gobby tools have a `consecutive-tool-b` restriction preventing rapid sequential tool calls. Multiple `call_tool` attempts in succession trigger cancellation errors with 'Cancelled: parallel tool call' message.

set_variable tool exists on FastMCP server backend but is NOT registered in Claude Code's deferred tools list, causing invisibility in CLI tool discovery despite being promoted to top-level MCP proxy tool in commit 0aa7440d. Registration gap exists in MCP proxy layer between server deployment and CLI client tool discovery.
When set_variable is not discoverable via CLI, use set_session_variable on gobby-workflows as equivalent workaround for session-scoped variable operations.

set_variable and get_variable tools currently indirect through call_tool in stdio server (pure HTTP proxy without session manager), while daemon version in server.py calls implementations directly via _set_var/_get_var. Planned refactor: Add HTTP endpoints /api/variables/set and /api/variables/get to daemon, extend DaemonProxy with proxy methods hitting these endpoints, register as top-level stdio tools (not discovered tools) to eliminate call_tool indirection.
GitHub MCP server may fail with credential errors; use 'gh' GitHub CLI as reliable fallback for querying repository issues and content

Issue #8 (by botbotfromuk) describes tick-based agent architecture: autonomous agent operating on ~60-second cycles (wake → check goals → execute tool calls → write trace/state to disk → sleep); requests integration with Gobby's session format and OpenTelemetry for batch-interval telemetry support

Two separate MCP server code paths: (1) create_mcp_server() in src/gobby/mcp_proxy/server.py creates the FastMCP for the HTTP daemon (used by internal tools), (2) create_stdio_mcp_server() + register_proxy_tools() in src/gobby/mcp_proxy/stdio.py creates the stdio MCP server that CLIs like Claude Code connect to via .mcp.json. Tools added to server.py are NOT automatically available to CLIs — they must also be added to stdio.py's register_proxy_tools(). The stdio proxy tools forward HTTP calls to the daemon via DaemonProxy.

mmkr: autonomous LLM-generated GitHub bot (created 2026-03-06, 13 repos, 0 stars) using tick-based scheduler with decision event logging. On 2026-03-07T09:17:03Z (tick 42), autonomously created GitHub issue on Gobby repo as scheduled action (not organic inquiry). Decision log contained reasoning: 'GobbyAI is a good target -- session handoff alignment'. Architecture parallel to gobby-cron.
