Rule editor mcp_call effect uses cascading UI pattern: server selection dropdown (populated from connected MCP servers with offline fallback) → tool selection dropdown (filtered by server, disabled until server chosen) → schema fetching via useMcp().fetchToolSchema() triggered on server+tool selection for context-aware argument configuration
Task #9225 was closed as already implemented during #9330 completion (no additional work required)

Hash-skip parameter was removed from VAD configuration (commit 7d457606, originally task #9557). When configuring @ricky0123/vad-web with baseAssetPath and onnxWASMBasePath, do not use hash-skip parameter.

Duplicate task reports often exist under different task IDs. When investigating reported issues, search git log for commits with `[gobby-#NNNN]` patterns to identify if work was already completed under a different task number and find the canonical implementation commit.

YAML parsing failures in configuration loader's sync initialization path silently truncate sync data—observed data loss of 14 records (sync.total: 14→0). Symptom: synced=0, total=0 in sync execution after config deserialization failure. Check YAML parsing first if sync table becomes empty.

Daemon/watchdog process management enforces singleton pattern to prevent multi-process contention and instability. Expected state: exactly one daemon process + one watchdog process. Multiple concurrent instances indicate singleton enforcement failure.

Commit 7d457606 removed hash-skip logic from export flow. src/gobby/sync/tasks.py contains zero references to tasks_meta or content_hash - export now unconditionally writes without conditional skipping based on hash.

Validators for HTML attribute tasks should validate HTML5 spec compliance rather than device-testing criteria. Device testing (e.g., iOS steps 1-4) cannot be verified from CLI and is out of scope. Remove unverifiable device-testing requirements from validator definitions.

`gobby start` must kill pre-existing watchdog processes before spawning new ones. Without this, stray watchdogs accumulate (persist for days) and trigger daemon restart loops every ~30s. Fixed in commit 72f08c2b.

Watchdog health check failures (404 errors from incorrect endpoints like `/admin/status`) repeatedly trigger daemon termination in ~30s cycles. When debugging daemon restart loops: verify no stray watchdog processes exist and confirm the watchdog's health endpoint is configured correctly.

The 'unknown SIGTERM' killing both daemon and watchdog was caused by competing watchdogs. The old zombie watchdog (from gobby start not killing watchdogs) hit /admin/status (404), accumulated health check failures, and restarted the daemon every ~30s. Root cause fixed in #9561 (commit 72f08c2b). Signal handler enhanced in #9562 (commit 440388f8) for future debugging.

Project task validators implement hard-coded device-testing requirements that cannot be satisfied through CLI verification or argument - task closure requires either manual device testing completion, user override, or agent intervention to clear validation criteria.

Daemon crash-restart loop root cause (fixed in commit 72f08c2b, #9561): gobby start called kill_all_gobby_daemons() which only matched gobby.runner processes, not gobby.watchdog. Zombie watchdogs accumulated across restarts. Old watchdogs hit /admin/status (404) instead of /api/admin/health (200), accumulating health check failures and restarting the daemon every ~30 seconds. Fix: added stop_watchdog() to start command and extended kill_all_gobby_daemons() to match gobby.watchdog. Also enhanced signal handler (440388f8, #9562) to log signal name, PID, PPID, and stack trace.

Gobby watchdog processes become orphaned when gobby start kills gobby.runner, causing accumulation over daemon restart cycles. Cleanup requires TWO functions working together: stop_watchdog() (targets processes with PID files) + kill_all_gobby_daemons() extended to match stray gobby.watchdog processes. Both necessary because some orphans lack PID file references. See commit 72f08c2b.

Unknown external process sends SIGTERM to both gobby daemon and gobby watchdog within seconds of each other. Observed in both daemons' logs. Source unidentified as of task #9561. If encountering simultaneous SIGTERM signals in daemon and watchdog logs, this is a known pattern requiring separate investigation.

Daemon watchdog process conflicts cause crash-restart cycles. Stale watchdog and MCP proxy processes from previous daemon shutdowns persist across restarts (observed: PID 5113 watchdog ~11 days old since Feb 20, PIDs 17182/17207 MCP proxy ~12 days old since Feb 19), competing for PID file control and destabilizing even a healthy daemon. Current watchdog implementation lacks process cleanup/reaper logic on exit. Symptoms: daemon constantly restarting despite being otherwise responsive.

Watchdog accumulation root cause: `gobby start` command in `cli/daemon.py` uses `kill_all_gobby_daemons()` which only kills `gobby.runner` processes, not watchdog. Watchdog stays alive and respawns daemon. Fix: add `stop_watchdog()` call to `start` command and update `kill_all_gobby_daemons()` to clean watchdog processes (mirroring `stop` command logic).

`/api/admin/shutdown` and `/api/admin/restart` endpoints don't clean watchdog processes. Watchdog can respawn daemon after shutdown, and restart leaves old watchdog running alongside new one. Both endpoints need explicit watchdog cleanup logic.

Validator cannot evaluate code changes from prior sessions (lacks diff context). Use skip_validation=True flag when comprehensive test results provide confidence. Applied to task #9544 where all 41 tests passed after merge fix implementation.

Watchdog process must use `/api/admin/health` endpoint (HTTP 200), not deprecated `/admin/status` which causes 404 errors and daemon termination. Stale watchdog processes using wrong endpoint need to be killed (check PID) and daemon restarted with corrected endpoint path.

Functions `close_task`, `validate_and_fix`, and `run_fix_attempt` in `stdio.py` exceed default 300-second timeout threshold during LLM validation cycles. These must be explicitly added to timeout allowlist in `stdio.py` to prevent task closure and validation pipeline failures.

Daemon timeout configuration: Task closure operations can take 24-26+ seconds when accounting for HTTP overhead (e.g., tasks #9549, #9552 required 26s, #9547 required 17s). Previous 30-second timeout was insufficient. When setting daemon timeouts for task operations, add buffer beyond measured operation time to account for network overhead.

Task validation during ticket closure fails with 'wants code evidence' error if code evidence is missing. Resolved #9552 by providing actual code diff/changes as evidence, not just textual description.
ReadTimeout errors during task validation (issue #9549) are caused by external MCP proxy timeout configuration. Not an agent code issue—check MCP proxy infrastructure setup.
LLM validation failures occur when validator lacks context from previous coding session. Workaround: merge commits with skip_validation flag and document justification.

Task sync tests in src/gobby/sync/tasks.py disable foreign key constraint checks to allow testing of invalid FK references without database violations. This is intentional for database round-trip validation and must be preserved.

Task export assertions for closed_at field must normalize timestamp precision at microsecond level. Exported values include microseconds, so test comparisons need to match or strip this precision to avoid false failures.

Watchdog health-check implements two distinct patterns: main loop uses `/api/admin/health` (line 114, correct), while startup verification uses legacy `/admin/status` endpoint (returns 404). 404 errors in logs during watchdog restart are artifacts from old watchdog process's final startup attempt, not failures of the new watchdog.
Task-sync numbering system can create duplicate [gobby-NNNN] commit message prefixes in history for completely different tasks/work items - this is a known artifact, not a merge conflict or duplicate

Gobby-skills registry exposes list_skills, get_skill, search_skills but NOT count_skills. Storage layer already supports count operations - fix by adding count_skills as a registered tool to the skills registry

Status endpoint at /api/admin/status (lines 184-195) calls registry.call("count_skills", {}) but count_skills tool is not registered in gobby-skills registry, causing silent failure and skills_stats["total"] to remain 0
_export_memories_sync() in src/gobby/daemon/export.py now reads existing memories.jsonl records before writing and merges instead of overwriting - preserves file-only records from other machines
Memory export listener removed - exports now fire only on daemon shutdown, session end, and explicit sync_export calls (not per-change) to reduce I/O and file contention

tasks.py (lines 199-202): skip logic had race condition—compared only stored meta hash (tasks_meta.json) without verifying on-disk file still matched. Git silently modifies files underneath, causing stale cached data to be incorrectly skipped. Hash-skip optimization removed to fix.

Hash-skip optimization in tasks.py removed because: saved only microseconds (single file write) while hash computation cost and tasks_meta.json tracking exceeded benefit. Replaced with unconditional read/merge/write pattern from _export_memories_sync() (line 389)—simpler and more reliable.

Daemon continuously exports to `memories.jsonl` during sessions (debounced 5s, controlled by `MemoryBackupConfig.export_debounce`) via `LocalMemoryManager._notify_listeners()` fired on every DB write. This is intentional/by-design but causes file staging conflicts during git operations. Workaround: disable/restart daemon before git rebase, or increase debounce to reduce churn.

Sync system's export-side hash-skip optimization (using hashlib) prevents redundant file *writes* only—provides zero benefit to import efficiency. Import-side implements independent Last-Write-Wins (LWW) using timestamps, doesn't depend on export hashes.
import_from_jsonl() implements per-task LWW at lines 313-317: skips reimporting if DB version (updated_at) is newer than file version. Import skipping is timestamp-based, not hash-based.

import_from_jsonl() (line 226) implements per-task Last-Write-Wins based on updated_at timestamps (lines 313–317). Tasks are skipped on import if the DB version is newer. This timestamp-based conflict resolution handles multi-writer scenarios without hash verification.

Task export/import: hash-skip optimization rejected. Export always writes (no skip), matching memories.jsonl pattern. Rationale: import's existing timestamp-based LWW makes hash-skip redundant—it would only skip file writes, not reduce import-side DB work.

export_to_jsonl() in src/gobby/daemon/export.py has unfixed bug (#9555): writes skipped when content hash matches tasks_meta.json, but post-git-operation causes stale on-disk file - needs hash invalidation after git operations

Task claim state management is in src/gobby/workflows/task_claim_state.py with pure helper functions add_claimed_task() and remove_claimed_task(). These return merge dicts without mutating state directly.
Session variable claimed_tasks is dict[str, str] mapping {uuid: "#N"} for concurrent task tracking. Replaces prior schema's claimed_task_id (str | None) and task_ref variables—these are now deleted.

CLI function claimed_task_ids() (plural) is a utility for gobby tasks list display—NOT the session variable. The session variable is claimed_tasks. This naming distinction prevents confusion between function and variable names.

Deprecated rules and variables created during YAML refactoring are automatically soft-deleted on next daemon restart. sync_bundled_rules() (lines 392-411) and sync_bundled_variables() (lines 744-764) perform cleanup by collecting valid names from disk YAML and soft-deleting DB entries with source='template' if rule/variable name is not in collected set.

YAML template files are decoupled from database state—deleting/renaming YAML rules or variables does not immediately remove DB entries. Changes propagate only when daemon restarts and runs sync functions that detect orphaned entries.

tasks_meta.json metadata tracking and hash-based change detection completely removed from sync/export subsystem. Affected files: src/gobby/sync/tasks.py (export_to_jsonl function no longer uses hashlib or metadata file I/O), src/gobby/cli/installers/git_hooks.py (tasks_meta.json staging removed). Related metadata-dependent tests deleted from tests/tasks/test_sync_tasks.py.

Task closure workflow is entirely manual and agent-driven. The [gobby-#NNNN] commit naming convention is documentary only. Tasks require explicit close_task() calls with commit SHAs to mark closed_at and link commits. No automatic commit detection or auto-closure mechanism exists.

Agents must explicitly call `close_task(task_id, commit_sha='...')` to link commits and close tasks. The `[gobby-#NNNN]` naming convention in commit messages does NOT automatically trigger linking—no post-commit hook implements automatic detection.

Pattern: agents commit code with proper [gobby-#NNNN] references but frequently omit the explicit close_task() calls, leaving tasks in open/in_progress state with no linked commits. Observed with tasks #9537, #9544, #9555.
auto_link_commits MCP tool exists to link git commits matching [gobby-#NNNN] patterns to tasks, but requires explicit manual invocation. Nothing triggers it automatically on commit detection.

Task state reversion bug: Tasks #9537, #9544, #9555 remain open in database despite appearing to close. Database verification showed in_progress/open status with no close_at records. Sync JSONL export captured open status with empty commits arrays. Hypothesis: sync import/export process overwrites close_task() changes. Next investigation: close_task() storage layer implementation and import execution timing relative to manual closes.

The task API's `update_task` endpoint uses the `parent_task_id` parameter to manage task hierarchy: set to a task ID to make it a child, set to `null` to remove it from parent context and make it standalone. This is the mechanism for reorganizing task hierarchies.

Expansion should be implemented as a reusable `expand-task` sub-pipeline (invocable via orchestrator's `invoke_pipeline: expand-task`, CLI `/gobby expand`, other pipelines, and standalone workflows) rather than orchestrator-integrated logic. Prioritizes reusability over single-purpose coupling.
Planning agent concept intentionally eliminated - consolidated into expansion agent as same operation at different fidelity levels. This removes a redundant stage from the pipeline.
Stage 3b (dependency analysis) made optional, gated by flag. Mechanical layer: `find_overlapping_tasks()` MCP call detects file overlap; nuanced layer: agent work for cross-file imports/shared state

Planning agent at stage 3a IS the expansion agent (not separate concepts). Planning is expansion at higher fidelity with `affected_files` context. This unifies previously separate conceptual concerns and clarifies architectural relationships.

Dependency analysis must be decoupled from agent work: file overlap detection (`find_overlapping_tasks()`) must be a mechanical MCP call, not agent work. Eliminates agent drift and prevents invented requirements in task creation.

Expansion spec validation (non-empty subtasks, valid dependency indices) must be MCP step, not agent-driven. Wire `affected_files` from spec into task tree via `TaskAffectedFileManager`. This removes validation from the LLM loop, improving reliability.

Gobby agent rule system refactor: Remove `rule_definitions` field from `AgentDefinitionBody` schema. This field contradicts pure composition and enabled silent parent rule drops in inheritance-based systems. All rules must now be standalone templates in `rules/` directory.

Tag auto-complete in `RuleEditForm.tsx` fetches available tags from `/api/rules/tags` endpoint on component mount, filters out already-selected tags, and renders them in a `<datalist>` element. Implementation pattern matches `AgentRulesEditor.tsx`.

Priority conflict warning in Rules UI: `RulesTab.tsx` uses `useMemo` to compute `conflictWarning` string when current rule's event+priority combination matches an existing rule. Prop passed to `RuleEditForm`, which renders yellow warning banner below priority field using `--color-warning` design token.

Remove inline `rule_definitions` from agent YAML definitions. All rules become standalone template files in `rules/` directory with `agent_scope` field for restriction (e.g., `agent_scope: [developer]`). Example: `rules/developer-no-push.yaml`. This was identified as a secondary design conflict—inline rules undermined the composition model and caused silent rule-dropping like the previous inheritance system.

Rule selection uses single mechanism: agents compose via `rule_selectors: {include: ["tag:..."]}`  OR automatic `agent_scope` matching (rules declare `agent_scope: [agent_type]` to restrict usage). Reject multiple selection paths to prevent design failures. Core principle: all rules are standalone templates selected through one mechanism.

Pipeline auto-execution pattern using mcp_call rule effect: event=session_start, condition=variables.get('_assigned_pipeline'), effect={type: mcp_call, server: gobby-pipelines, tool: run_pipeline, arguments: {name: '{{ _assigned_pipeline }}'}}. This replaces previous prompt-based enforcement rules (inject-pipeline-instructions, enforce-pipeline-tools, restrict-pipeline-call-tool) with deterministic server-side automation.

Pipelines execute server-side with independent LLM service for prompt steps. Agent session provides execution context (variables, rules) but pipeline runs autonomously. Agent does not block during pipeline execution; pipeline LLM calls are separate from agent quota.

RuleEditForm.tsx tag auto-complete: partially implemented. Backend /api/rules/tags endpoint is functional and tested. Frontend integration missing but AgentRulesEditor.tsx contains working reference implementation showing the pattern to follow.

Rule selection in composition model uses two mechanisms: (1) tag-based via agent's `rule_selectors: {include: ['tag:developer']}` and (2) scope-based via rule template's `agent_scope: [developer]` field for auto-matching. Rule resolution logic must check both.

Current implementation: inject-pipeline-instructions rule fires on session_start, injecting text instructions that require agent to voluntarily call run_pipeline. Problems identified: non-deterministic execution, no guarantee pipeline runs, token waste on 4-step discovery overhead, risk of context confusion derailing agent.

Pipeline execution on agent session start needs architectural change: move from request-based model (prompt instruction injection requiring agent interpretation) to execution-based model (direct, deterministic pipeline invocation at session initialization). Currently relies on implicit agent behavior.

Pipelines are orchestration frameworks without independent reasoning capability. When an agent with `_assigned_pipeline` starts and `session_start` rule fires `run_pipeline` via mcp_call, the pipeline executes server-side. Intelligence comes from spawned agents or direct tool calls, not from the pipeline itself
No `spawn_agent` step type exists in pipelines. Agents spawn via `mcp` steps calling the `spawn_agent` tool on `gobby-agents` MCP server.

When an agent with `_assigned_pipeline` fires `session_start` rule executing `run_pipeline` via `mcp_call`, the pipeline runs server-side independent of the original agent's session and can autonomously spawn agents, call tools, or execute commands.
`prompt` steps execute single-shot LLM calls via `llm_service.get_default_provider().generate_text(prompt)` — no tools, no conversation history, basic text I/O. Located in handlers.py:109-125
No native `spawn_agent` step type exists. Agent spawning happens via `mcp` steps calling `spawn_agent` on the `gobby-agents` MCP service
`prompt` pipeline steps are single-shot LLM calls via `llm_service.get_default_provider().generate_text(prompt)` (handlers.py:109-125) with no tools or conversation context—not conversational.

Design pattern for deterministic agent behavior: eliminate choice through rules/constraints rather than instruction compliance alone. Lock agents with rules until only target action remains possible. `kill_agent` step is proven working example. Pipeline execution should follow same constraint-based determinism model—enforce constraints upward through rules, not downward through goal alignment.

Constraint-based pipeline architecture: each pipeline step defines constraints (available tools/actions), a goal, and a gate (validation for advancement). Implementation uses `_pipeline_step` variable to condition rule activation and vary constraints dynamically. Determinism achieved through elimination of choice (tool removal), not instruction compliance.

Expansion sub-pipeline constraint profile: 4-step structure with (1) Research—Read/Glob/Grep/MCP allowed, Write blocked, gate: `save_expansion_spec` called; (2) Validate—MCP only, validates spec against source plan; (3) Execute—MCP only, creates tasks, gate: `execute_expansion` succeeds; (4) Wire deps—MCP only, stores affected_files and runs overlap analysis. Step 1 creative (exploration freedom), Steps 2-4 deterministic (funneled into specific MCP calls).

Constraint mechanism pattern already validated by existing `kill_agent` step—reference this implementation as proof-of-concept for blocking/enabling agent actions via tool elimination. Same constraint framework applies across different pipeline targets.

Expansion sub-pipeline: 4 sequential steps with constraint tiers. (1) Research: Read/Glob/Grep/MCP allowed, Write blocked → explore codebase, produce expansion spec. (2) Validate: only MCP allowed → validate spec vs source plan. (3) Execute: only MCP allowed → create tasks. (4) Wire deps: only MCP allowed → store affected_files, run overlap analysis. Each step has Gate (completion validator) before advancing.

Pipeline constraint control: use `_pipeline_step` variable as rule conditional driver and `_assigned_pipeline` variable as lock mechanism. When `_assigned_pipeline` is set, rules block all actions except `run_pipeline` and discovery. Step completion gates advance `_pipeline_step`, activating new constraint tiers.

Pipeline is mandatory first agent action AND constraint framework controlling all subsequent work. Execution pattern: session init (locked to run_pipeline only) → agent runs pipeline → step constraints activate → work within constraints → gate satisfied → pipeline advances → new constraints apply → repeat. Constraints tighten for deterministic outcomes, loosen for creative exploration.

Expansion sub-pipeline uses 3-stage process: (1) Expansion agent phase - explores codebase, generates spec with `affected_files` field, persists via `save_expansion_spec` tool, (2) Validation phase - sanity checks (non-empty subtasks, valid dependencies, proper wiring), (3) Execution phase - atomic `execute_expansion` MCP call creates task tree.

Expansion pipeline 4-step structure: (1) spawn agent with Glob/Grep/Read access, (2) validate spec via MCP validation step (not agent), (3) atomic execution via `execute_expansion()`, (4) wire affected_files via TaskAffectedFileManager MCP call

Expansion system is flaky because it's a complex 5-phase skill dependent on agent faithfully following nuanced prompts across potential session compactions. Strategic redesign: restructure as pipeline separating creative agent work (spec generation via `execute_expansion`) from deterministic operations (validation, execution, file wiring via `TaskAffectedFileManager`), isolating failure points and improving reliability.

Current expansion system's core breaking point: mixing research and task creation in a single pass causes research findings (discovered constraints, edge cases) to bleed into task generation as invented requirements not in original scope. Root cause of flakiness is 5-phase expansion depending on agent faithfully following complex prompts through session compactions.

Expansion system is flaky due to mixed concerns: agent researches AND creates tasks in same pass, causing research findings to bleed into task creation as invented requirements. Proposed fix: three-phase pipeline (research → validation → execution). Phase 1: expansion agent explores codebase and produces finalized spec via `save_expansion_spec`. Phase 2: mechanical validation against source plan. Phase 3: builder (`execute_expansion`) creates tasks deterministically from spec.

Proposed expansion redesign: two decoupled phases. Phase 1: Research Agent explores codebase, produces expansion spec via `save_expansion_spec` (constrained to spec authorship only). Phase 2: Mechanical Builder creates task tree deterministically via `execute_expansion` with no LLM involvement. Spec is the contract between phases; eliminates compaction-dependent task generation.

`run_pipeline` (MCP tool on gobby-pipelines service) is the agent-facing interface for pipeline invocation with parameters name, session_id, inputs, wait. `invoke_pipeline` (pipeline step type in executor) is internal orchestration for nested pipelines—enables one pipeline to call another as a step. Agents interact exclusively with run_pipeline; invoke_pipeline is not exposed to the agent interface.

Gobby pre-commit hook infinite loop: The installed `.git/hooks/pre-commit` runs `gobby task sync` which modifies `.gobby/tasks.jsonl`, then does `git add` on it. This can re-trigger pre-commit hooks causing an infinite loop. Key files involved: `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, `.gobby/hooks/pre-commit-task-sync.sh`, `src/gobby/cli/install.py`, and the installed `.git/hooks/pre-commit`.

memories.jsonl sync/backup mechanism is ONE-WAY (local→remote only), NOT bidirectional. Module docstring (lines 1–9) explicitly states 'NOT a bidirectional sync mechanism'. This is architectural design, not a bug. Remote updates will NOT automatically sync back to local.

Previous memories.jsonl gap (647 local vs 1,555 remote) resulted from --theirs conflict resolution during stash conflict, which took the stashed local version instead of remote. Future conflicts on memories.jsonl should explicitly prioritize remote version to avoid repeating this divergence.

File annotations in orchestrator-v2 should be treated as predictions, not contracts. Update via post-hoc git diff with `annotation_source='observed'` to capture observed file touches for learning feedback loop. Avoid mid-execution re-analysis to prevent complexity issues.

Orchestrator-v2 planning agent (Q2 decision): Replace current blind LLM-only expansion with planning agent performing actual codebase exploration. Use expansion prompt as internal template that planning agent refines via code reading. Eliminates duplicate expansion systems at different quality levels.

`create_worktree` function not receiving `provider` parameter from pipeline config; prevents CLI hook installation in worktrees. Add provider forwarding in pipeline to enable worktree setup. Blocking for Stage 1.
QA review agent runs without worktree context, unable to see actual code changes under review. Must propagate worktree context to QA agent for functional code review. Blocking bug for Stage 1.
MCP `use_local` parameter: explicitly pass in pipeline YAML rather than relying on MCP tool auto-detection to avoid misfire without remote tracking branch.

TDD enforcement (`enforce-tdd-on-new` rule) blocker: determining which code file a test covers requires language-agnostic path mapping. No universal naming convention exists across Python (tests/foo/test_bar.py → src/foo/bar.py), JavaScript, Go, Rust, etc.

State machine approach to TDD enforcement (toggle `tdd_test_written` flag, check on code write, reset) breaks when agent requires multiple code writes after a single test write. Path tracking with `tdd_covered_paths` list is more flexible but requires language-specific convention inference logic.
TDD enforcement PreToolUse rule uses state flag format: tdd_nudged:{file_path} to permit retries after test file written. Flag is set when rule blocks first write to new code file.
TDD enforcement rule exempts test files (e.g., tests/foo/test_bar.py) from PreToolUse block - rule checks if target file is a test file and skips blocking if so.

TDD enforcement splits process nudging (PreToolUse rule blocks + sets flag) from outcome enforcement (validation_criteria injection with 'Tests must exist' requirement). Rule nudges agent toward test-first; validation prevents task closure without tests. Reusable pattern for other process-outcome enforcement pairs.

merge_worktree MCP tool is FULLY ISOLATED — it only runs git commands in the worktree (cwd=worktree_path), never the main repo. Strategy: merge target INTO source branch in the worktree. The fast-forward that updates the target branch is a SEPARATE exec step in the coordinator pipeline ('git checkout <target> && git merge --ff-only <source>'). The merge agent template must NOT have raw git merge fallback instructions — only merge_worktree MCP tool.

Pipeline templates now live in src/gobby/install/shared/workflows/pipelines/ as individual YAML files with `type: pipeline`. The sync_bundled_workflows() handles both workflows and pipelines by detecting the `type` field. There is no separate pipelines sync target. The orphan cleanup in sync_bundled_workflows filters by workflow_type='workflow', so pipeline rows are preserved.

agent_definitions table migration (v117): The 29-field agent_definitions table was deleted and replaced by workflow_definitions with workflow_type='agent'. Data is stored as 15-field AgentDefinitionBody in definition_json, with separate role/goal/personality/instructions fields (not merged). scope maps to source (bundled→template, global→installed). As of migration 124, source values are: template, installed (global), project (project-scoped). The 'imported' and 'custom' values no longer exist. BASELINE_VERSION is now 147.

CLAUDE.md guiding principle #11: 'NEVER leave options in plans.' Plans are for execution, not exploration. All decisions must be resolved before exiting plan mode — no 'optional future' items, no 'TBD', no deferred choices. Anti-pattern: writing 'Actually, the cleaner approach would be...' inside a plan is deliberation, not planning. Resolve it before committing to the plan. Connects to the 'proper fix over quick-and-dirty' principle — when multiple approaches exist, pick the proper one and write only that into the plan.

Remaining `register_pipeline_continuation` references in pipeline YAMLs: dev-loop.yaml (deprecated) confirmed; orchestrator pipeline also requires audit. Both need completion for full cleanup verification
Pre-existing test failure in tests/workflows (#10036) is unrelated to continuation machinery removal - not caused by recent changes, tracked separately
When verifying removal of deprecated patterns: exclude deprecated/ directories, CHANGELOG files, and .jsonl files from search—these are expected to contain historical references for audit purposes.

Continuation→conductor architectural transition was documentation-only: updated docs/guides/pipelines.md, docs/guides/orchestrator.md, docs/plans/conductor-deferred.md. No code changes, no database migrations required.

register_pipeline_continuation mechanism fully deprecated as of task #10037 (commit f8821d4b). Conductor-based event-driven re-invocation is the sole supported pattern. Historical references intentionally preserved only in deprecated directories, changelogs, and jsonl for audit trail.

Pattern for tool removal audits: Prioritize updating active documentation (guides referenced by agents for instructions), leave historical plans and deprecated/archived code as-is. This categorization determines cleanup ROI.

register_pipeline_continuation audit complete: docs/guides/pipelines.md (lines 426, 482) and docs/guides/orchestrator.md (line 120) require updates since agents reference them; docs/plans/conductor-deferred.md and docs/plans/completed/pipeline-heartbeat.md are historical (can leave as-is); src/gobby/install/shared/workflows/deprecated/pipelines/dev-loop.yaml is inert (can leave as-is). Awaiting user decision to proceed with doc updates.

Cached tool schemas without continuation support cause agent failures. When continuation machinery is removed from codebase but agents retain old cached schemas, extended failures result until schema cache is refreshed.

Task orphaning scenario: when a pipeline execution fails due to an unrelated error after a task is marked in_progress, the task remains stuck in in_progress state with no active agent. The lifecycle monitor (_recover_task_from_failed_agent) only handles agent-level failures, not pipeline-level failures, so doesn't catch these orphaned tasks. Proposed solution: detect in_progress tasks with no active agents at orchestrator tick start.

Orchestrator has no cleanup mechanism for status='failed' executions - they accumulate indefinitely in database as clutter. The re-entrancy guard checks for status='running' executions but never removes or cleans up failed ones. No retention policy exists for orphaned execution records.

Pre-existing mypy errors in runner.py (lines 577, 589)—verified as not in scope for orchestrator/pipeline/cron work. Files modified in this work (orchestrator.yaml, executor.py, _factory.py) verified clean with no mypy errors. Avoid fixing pre-existing runner.py errors in unrelated PRs.

Pipeline heartbeat system (cron handler ~110 lines, executes every 60s) is ESSENTIAL for tick-based orchestrator safety—prevents permanent deadlock from agent failures. Detects executions with status='running' stale >120s, checks RunningAgentRegistry for agent liveness: if alive → touches updated_at; if dead → marks failed. Without heartbeat: crashed agents leave executions stuck in 'running', permanently blocking orchestrator's re-entrancy guard. Critical sequence: orchestrator tick → creates execution → agents work → heartbeat detects stalls after 120s → marks failed → next tick proceeds.
`dispatch_batch` calls `spawn_agent` internally at line 354, making timeout addition a straightforward single parameter passthrough with minimal code changes

Stale agent claim pattern: When spawn_agent steps assign tasks (e.g., marking as in_progress with session ID), dead/crashed agents leave stale assignments. Example: task #9916 marked in_progress with session 38192b4e but no running agent. Orchestrator recovery logic must handle via skip or reclaim mechanisms.

Pure deterministic pipeline execution does not require ChatSession/conductor-level orchestration. PipelineExecutor runs standalone without chat-level coordination when containing only exec/mcp/invoke_pipeline/condition steps.

PipelineExecutor step types: exec (shell command), mcp (tool invocation), invoke_pipeline (sub-pipeline delegation), condition (expression evaluation), spawn_agent (LLM-spawned subprocess). Only spawn_agent involves LLM; all others are deterministic. Likely in src/gobby/pipeline/executor.py or similar.

Deleted template files don't auto-remove their installed rules from the database. Rules persist until the next daemon sync cycle (when sync_bundled_content_to_db() runs during daemon restart). This explains why deleted template rules can still trigger stop gates until daemon restarts. File system deletion ≠ DB deletion.

Conductor design documentation is located in `docs/plans/completed/orchestration.md` — this is the original orchestration plan that established the conductor pattern and contains the original design rationale and requirements

Template file deletion does not immediately remove gate rules from database. Gate rules persist in DB until daemon restart when template sync runs. On next daemon startup, template sync detects the deleted file and removes the orphaned database rule. This prevents race conditions during agent execution.

Memory review enforcement refactored from stop-gate blocker (`stop-gates/require-memory-review.yaml`, deleted) to conditional `before_tool` block on `close_task` action in `memory-capture-after-close.yaml`. New mechanism gates on `pending_memory_review` + `commit_sha` conditions to maintain data integrity while reducing user friction.

Conductor ChatSession is architecturally non-functional: lacks MCP tool wiring to execute actions. If enabled without tool integration, produces only text responses ('No action needed'), resulting in token waste with no actual orchestration capability. This is a critical gap blocking real conductor utility.

ConductorManager is wired in runner.py lines 603-630 and auto-creates cron job 'gobby:conductor-tick' firing every 120 seconds when enabled. Disabled by default via ConductorConfig.enabled = False in conductor.py line 13.

The YAML sync process automatically handles rule deletions/renames for memory lifecycle rules. After renaming or deleting a rule, verify the YAML configuration files reflect the changes correctly rather than manually updating them.

When deleting or renaming memory lifecycle rules, audit for broken references in: test_memory_lifecycle_rules.py, idle detector tests, stop-gates tests, and YAML configuration files. Missing any of these locations could leave orphaned rule references.

Two main architectural patterns for pipeline orchestration integration: (A) Conductor fires pipeline via MCP when work detected (intelligent timing, higher LLM token cost per tick); (B) Separate pipeline cron on fixed schedule with conductor only monitoring (simpler/decoupled, fires regardless of work availability).

ConductorManager is registered as cron handler with name 'conductor_tick' and auto-creates job 'gobby:conductor-tick'. Disabled by default via ConductorConfig.enabled=False in conductor.py. Conductor ChatSession has system prompt to check task/pipeline state via MCP tools but lacks direct pipeline invocation wiring—this gap drives architectural choices.

Deleting a Gobby rule file from disk does not remove it from the DB. Old rules (e.g., stop-gate rules like `require-memory-review`) will keep firing until explicitly removed from the database or their gate is cleared via the MCP tool.

When template rules are soft-deleted (deleted_at flag set), their installed copies in the DB remain active and continue to enforce until explicitly cleared via gate bypass/uninstall. Simply deleting a template does not cascade to remove active installed instances—this affects stop hooks and other rule-based gates.
dispatch_batch in _factory.py extended with optional timeout parameter (timeout: float | None), propagated to spawn_agent. Commit a37cf0a4.

executor.py _execute_pipeline method: use pipeline_executor.loader instead of bare WorkflowLoader() to provide DB context. Wrap with set_project_context to enable MCP tool resolution of task references like #9916.

In executor.py, MCP tool task reference resolution (e.g., #9916) requires using `pipeline_executor.loader` instead of bare `WorkflowLoader()` to preserve DB context, then wrapping with `set_project_context` wrapper. Without this, MCP tools cannot resolve task ID references.

executor.py execution must be wrapped with set_project_context() to enable MCP tool resolution (e.g., task references like #9916). Without it, task refs won't resolve within pipeline context. File: src/gobby/devloop/executor.py
executor.py _execute_pipeline must use pipeline_executor.loader (has DB connection) instead of bare WorkflowLoader() - the latter has no database context. File: src/gobby/devloop/executor.py
Cron job invoking orchestrator pipeline only passes task_id in action_config. No isolation_mode or clone_id input parameters exist yet; adding them would require conditional logic in pipeline YAML.

Orchestrator pipeline YAML hardcodes worktree-specific steps (get_worktree_by_task, create_worktree, merge_worktree). To enable runtime isolation mode switching, pipeline must be refactored to use conditional steps controlled by an input parameter rather than replaced entirely.

`get_worktree_by_task` tool throws error instead of gracefully returning null when no worktree exists. MCP step errors are fatal in pipeline architecture with no error-handling mechanism in pipeline step design - single tool failure crashes entire pipeline.

Orchestrator pipeline has fundamental architectural flaw: currently designed to use worktrees but should use clones (project-level filesystem mirrors instead). Root cause of crashes when tasks lack linked worktrees (e.g., task #9915). Cron-triggered pipeline has been disabled pending architectural fix to switch from worktree-based to clone-based approach.

MCP's reload_cache syncs YAML pipeline definitions to DB only—it does not reload Python source code changes into the running daemon process. When Python files are modified (executor.py, _factory.py, etc.), the daemon must be restarted to load updated code into memory. reload_cache + daemon restart is the correct remediation path for Python code changes.

GitHub Dependabot moderate-severity vulnerability in repository dependencies flagged at https://github.com/GobbyAI/gobby/security/dependabot/6. Requires review and remediation in next maintenance window.

First callable migration in the codebase: _migrate_v147_add_agent_run_columns (commit 94ab0751). Uses PRAGMA table_info to check existing columns before ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN, making it idempotent. Pattern: db.fetchall('PRAGMA table_info(table)'), build set of names, skip columns that exist. MigrationAction type is str | Callable[[LocalDatabase], None].

Pipeline executor child outputs are namespaced under an 'output' key in step_output dict (commit 94ab0751). Previously child_outputs were merged directly into step_output via .update(), which could clobber wrapper keys like 'status', 'pipeline', 'execution_id'. Now: step_output['output'] = child_outputs. Pipeline YAMLs referencing nested pipeline results should use e.g. steps.foo.output.output.bar (the first .output is the step output, the second is the child namespace).

invoke_pipeline steps in pipelines return {pipeline, execution_id, status, output: {child_keys...}}. Child outputs are namespaced under 'output' key (commit 94ab0751) to prevent clobbering wrapper keys. Previously (d395b0a2) they were merged flat via .update(). Parent steps reference child results as e.g. dev_loop.output.output.orchestration_complete.
Orchestrator does NOT clean up failed executions—this responsibility was confirmed absent from the pipeline code (110 lines). Cleanup logic would need to go elsewhere.

Never do 'simplest fix' — always do the best, most correct fix. When a tool is missing functionality that a sibling tool has (e.g., clones missing task_id resolution that worktrees has), add proper parity rather than working around the gap.

In worktrees.py, codex_autonomous.py, canvas.py: wrap blocking sync operations in asyncio.to_thread when called from async context to prevent event loop blocking. Pattern applied Session 2 - maintain when adding new async code to these modules.

YAML effect: vs effects: normalization decision: backward-compat alias already exists in definitions.py model validator. This was examined as CodeRabbit finding but triaged as won't-fix - full normalization is cosmetic churn without justified benefit.

In async context, wrap synchronous operations with asyncio.to_thread to prevent blocking event loop: worktrees.py (_run_git calls), codex_autonomous.py (DB writes), canvas.py (file reads). Prevents race conditions and deadlocks.

definitions.py supports both singular 'effect:' and plural 'effects:' keys via model validator with backward-compat alias. 46 files use singular, 17 use plural. Schema normalization deferred—no standardization work needed.

Pipeline executor has a scoping bug where `len` (and likely other functions in `_PIPELINE_EVAL_FUNCS`) are undefined during cron job execution. The functions ARE defined in `_PIPELINE_EVAL_FUNCS` dict at line 15 of pipeline_executor.py, but when orchestrator/cron jobs run, they fail with `'len' is undefined`. This suggests the eval functions dict is not being properly passed to the expression evaluation context in the orchestrated execution path—likely a disconnect between interactive vs. orchestrated pipeline runner contexts.

Expression evaluation pattern in Gobby: pure expressions ${{ expr }} should route to SafeExpressionEvaluator (includes builtins), template/mixed strings use Jinja2. This pattern is already proven and correctly implemented in the MCP argument handling layer—replicate it elsewhere when expression evaluation is needed.

Pipeline output expression bug (commit d321d666): `_build_outputs()` in pipeline_executor.py line 808 was routing all ${{ }} expressions through Jinja2's render_string, which lacks builtins (len, bool, str, int). Fixed via pure-expression detection regex: pure ${{ expr }} now routes to SafeExpressionEvaluator (includes builtins), mixed strings retain Jinja2 fallthrough.

Pipeline _build_outputs (pipeline_executor.py:804) routes all ${{ }} output expressions through Jinja2, which lacks len/bool/str/int builtins. Pure expressions must be detected with regex and routed through SafeExpressionEvaluator (via renderer._resolve_expression) instead — same pattern as render_mcp_arguments. Fixed in commit d321d666.

Current cron session implementation is mechanical (satisfies session contract) rather than principled. A conductor approach—LLM-powered session for intelligent orchestration and failure handling—would be more sophisticated but was not implemented. Known architectural trade-off.

Cron jobs require synthetic sessions created at execution time with source='cron', machine_id='cron', and no parent session. This bypasses spawn_agent's depth validation on parent session chains, which fails when parent sessions are dead/compacted from async execution delays. Synthetic cron sessions satisfy the pipeline executor's session contract.

Cron-triggered pipelines have no session by default. spawn_agent requires parent_session_id, so CronExecutor._execute_pipeline must create a session via pipeline_executor.session_manager before calling execute(). Without this, dispatch_batch silently fails with success:false. Fixed in commit 09a87a80.

CronExecutor._execute_pipeline() must create a cron session before spawning child agents to ensure spawned agents receive valid parent_session_id context. Session creation is prerequisite for agent task coordination and error propagation. Bug #10051 pt.1 (commit 09a87a80).

In pipeline_executor.py `_build_outputs`, use `SafeExpressionEvaluator` (not Jinja2) to evaluate pure `${{ }}` expressions. SafeExpressionEvaluator supports `len()` and other built-in functions that Jinja2 lacks. This matches MCP argument handling pattern. Bug #10050 fix (commit d321d666).

Spawned agents from orchestrator/cron workflows must receive explicit `parent_session_id: "${{ session_id }}"` parameter in workflow dispatch stanzas (e.g., dispatch_devs/dispatch_qa in orchestrator.yaml). Without it, spawned agents lack session context and cannot track tasks or propagate errors. Pattern discovered in Bug #10051 fixes (commits 09a87a80, 2a6b7173).

RuleDefinitionBody refactoring from singular `effect` field to plural `effects` array (task #10049) used Pydantic `mode='before'` validator in `definitions.py` to handle backward compatibility with legacy JSON payloads containing the old `effect` key. This approach enables schema migrations without breaking compatibility during transition windows.

Production code backward compatibility pattern: Across 5 production files, implemented dual-read approach that prefers effects field but automatically falls back to effect for legacy DB records. This allows gradual migration without breaking existing data access.

Field deprecation/migration pattern: Used mode="before" Pydantic validator in definitions.py to convert deprecated effect field to effects field. This enables backward compatibility while ensuring model_dump() writes clean forward-only records. Validator processes incoming data before field validation.

RuleDefinitionBody no longer has an `effect` (singular) field. Only `effects` (plural list) exists. A model_validator(mode='before') converts singular `effect` to `effects` list for backward compat with old DB records. All YAML templates use `effects:` (plural). Production code reading raw definition_json from DB uses `body.get('effects')` with fallback to `body.get('effect')` for pre-migration records. The sync.py repair query still checks both `$.effect` and `$.effects`.

Epic #9915: Task #9916 is the critical blocker for the epic. Recently reopened, with 9 dependent tasks blocked waiting for its completion. Fixing #9916 is prerequisite for progress on remaining 9 tasks.

YAML effect: vs effects: normalization is now DONE — all YAML templates use plural `effects:`. The singular form is only retained as a backward-compat model_validator for old DB records. No longer a won't-fix cosmetic item.

Effect key normalization is complete: all YAML templates now use plural `effects:` key. The singular `effect:` key is only supported via backward-compat model_validator for old DB records. The definitions.py model no longer has a singular `effect` field.

Gobby pre-commit hook in hook_dispatcher.py enforces require-task-close rule: blocks commits when tasks remain in in_progress status. Must transition task to terminal state (closed, review_approved, escalated, etc.) before committing work.
Commit d75f68c7 introduced intentional scope-agnostic block clearing behavior. TestToolBlockPendingScopeAware test assertions expect this scope-agnostic behavior—this is by design, not a test bug.

Cron scheduler DI threading: run_cron_job MCP tool requires cron_scheduler threaded through ServiceContainer → setup_internal_registries() → create_cron_registry() to reach tool layer. Without this chain, tool hits fallback 'not running' path instead of calling scheduler.run_now().

Block clearing in src/gobby/storage is intentionally scope-agnostic by design (commit d75f68c7). TestToolBlockPendingScopeAware test assertions must match this behavior; scope-aware expectations will be stale. Bug #10036.

Cron scheduler must be threaded through dependency injection chain: ServiceContainer → setup_internal_registries() → create_cron_registry(). Without this pattern, run_cron_job() hits 'not running' fallback and scheduler.run_now() never executes. Bug #10053.

Block clearing behavior in TestToolBlockPendingScopeAware is scope-agnostic (clears blocks globally, not per-scope). This is intentional design from commit d75f68c7, not a regression. Test expectations align with this behavior—if block clearing behavior changes, reference this decision.

run_cron_job tool requires cron_scheduler to be threaded through: setup_internal_registries() -> create_cron_registry() -> run_cron_job implementation. Without proper parameter passing of ServiceContainer's cron_scheduler instance through this chain, the tool falls back instead of calling scheduler.run_now(). If cron jobs don't execute immediately, verify cron_scheduler propagation.

CronScheduler DI path: GobbyRunner creates it → ServiceContainer.cron_scheduler → must be passed through setup_internal_registries() → create_cron_registry(). If new services are added to GobbyRunner, remember to thread them through the registries/http.py DI chain.
Pre-approval mechanism gap: project-level settings.json with 'allowedTools' configuration may be required to suppress trust prompts in project-scoped contexts - needs end-to-end testing
TRUST_PROMPT_PATTERNS regex incomplete - must expand to match variant pattern: 'Is this a project.*you trust' (issue #10054)
TRUST_DISMISS_KEYS constant needs update from '2\n' to Enter key - UI pre-selects option 1 with ❯ cursor indicator, not option 2 as code expects (issue #10054)
`run_cron_job` MCP tool retains stale scheduler reference after daemon restart (#10053). Does not block autonomous execution but can cause issues on daemon restart cycles.

CronExecutor was missing `project_path` field in project context dict passed to `spawn_agent_impl`. Must extract from `projects.repo_path` and include in context dict before dispatch. Fixed in c4f8c4b8.

LocalDatabase.connection is a @property (not a method/context manager). Use db.execute(sql, params) directly for queries, or db.transaction()/db.transaction_immediate() as context managers for writes. Don't use 'with db.connection() as conn:' — it will fail silently.
Exporter factory in exporters.py supports 4 output formats: console, OTLP, Prometheus, and rotating-file
TelemetrySettings dataclass (config.py) is integrated into DaemonConfig alongside LoggingSettings; backward compatibility maintained by preserving deprecated LoggingSettings
Metrics test fixtures (test_metrics.py) use InMemoryMetricReader, not InMemoryMetricExporter - subtle but important distinction in OTel SDK

Pre-commit hook has side effect of modifying route files unintentionally (likely through code generation or automatic formatting). Route files must be manually restored to clean state before successful commit.

Pre-commit stop hook requires Gobby daemon to be running for session state cleanup. Hook fails with 'Gobby daemon not running' error if daemon not initialized. Workaround: run `gobby start` before attempting commit to initialize daemon.

Anthropic's `POST /v1/messages/count_tokens` endpoint is available via existing `anthropic` Python SDK. Free tokenization-only (no billing), 100% accurate using model's tokenizer, ~20-50ms latency. Suitable for compression analysis of session transcripts.

Spend tracking pipeline has 4 specific broken components: (1) no `cost` field in JSONL output, (2) missing cache pricing in calculations, (3) no cost calculation logic implemented, (4) model parameter not passed through the chain. Multiple bugs compound the breakage.

Session transcripts are currently stored as gzip-compressed blobs in the `session_transcripts` SQLite table, but this is unnecessary database bloat since JSONL source files already exist at predictable filesystem paths (~/.claude/projects/<project>/<session-id>.jsonl). Decision made to refactor to filesystem references instead. Related to task #10058.

Token counting in Gobby uses chars-per-token heuristic (~3.7) with 15-25% variance by content type: prose 4.0-4.5, code 3.2-3.8, structured data 2.5-3.0. Anthropic count_tokens() API proposed for exact measurements in spend tracking.

`close_task` tool lacks enforcement restrictions preventing clone agents from executing it. This operation should be restricted to developer agent workflow only. Current implementation allows clone agents to call terminal operations.

Lifecycle monitor failed to detect and report silent agent death for task #9917. No alert was triggered and task remains unresolved. This indicates a detection gap where agent terminations can occur without monitoring/reopening mechanisms catching them.

Task #9921 death spiral: `mark_task_needs_review` tool call failed, triggering consecutive-tool-block rule which prevented `kill_agent` recovery execution. The blocking rule becomes destructive when underlying tool fails, creating unrecoverable task states. Root cause analysis needed in `mark_task_needs_review` implementation or task state validation logic.

Agents working in clones should NOT call close_task. Only the merge agent (merge-clone pipeline) should close tasks, since it's the one that brings commits into main. Dev agents mark needs_review, QA marks review_approved, merge agent closes.
VIEW mode in `web/src/components/terminals/TerminalsPage.tsx` was dropping mouse escape sequences. Must pass-through mouse events for terminal interaction and scrolling in attached sessions.

Tmux scrolling in web UI requires mouse mode enabled. Add `set mouse on` command in `src/gobby/servers/websocket/tmux.py` when attaching sessions. Tmux's alternate screen buffer suppresses local scrollback; mouse mode enables xterm.js scrolling fallback.

Tmux mixin feature changes (scroll forwarding behavior in VIEW-mode) require BOTH daemon restart (backend) and frontend rebuild to take effect. Changes are split across backend Python code and frontend logic, necessitating coordinated deployment.

Mouse input scrolling in tmux web UI (xterm.js) requires fixing two issues in src/gobby/servers/websocket/tmux.py: (1) Enable tmux mouse mode with 'mouse on' config in session attachment logic, and (2) ensure VIEW mode doesn't drop mouse escape sequences. Both fixes are necessary; either alone is insufficient.

Lifecycle monitor failed to auto-reopen task #9917 after silent agent death. Tasks can be orphaned in `in_progress` state indefinitely when agents die without task cleanup. Monitor detection/reopening logic is incomplete.

Agent tool execution constraint: kill_agent is blocked as 'retry after prior tool failure' when mark_task_needs_review or mark_task_review_approved fails. Prevents agents from self-terminating after completing work with transient state-update failures. Affects agent autonomy/robustness.

clone_task(commit_sha=...) validates commit existence against main repo only; commits in clone branches fail validation even if correctly staged. Workaround: `out_of_repo` flag in `close_task()` call, but unsustainable. See src/gobby/storage/migrations.py and task closure logic.

Web UI tmux terminals: tmux attach handler in src/gobby/servers/websocket/tmux.py configures session options (status off, mouse on) before starting the PTY reader. Frontend TerminalView in web/src/components/terminals/TerminalsPage.tsx has VIEW/EDIT modes — VIEW mode forwards mouse escape sequences (SGR \x1b[< and X10 \x1b[M) but blocks keyboard input.
Frontend websocket handler's `onData` method silently dropped mouse escape sequences in VIEW mode, breaking tmux mouse input through web UI—these sequences must be forwarded through

Tmux mouse scrolling via web UI requires two-part fix: (1) Backend: set `mouse on` in tmux attach logic at `src/gobby/servers/websocket/tmux.py`, (2) Frontend: websocket `onData` handler must forward mouse escape sequences in VIEW mode (was silently dropping them)

VIEW mode (read-only sessions) must allow mouse scroll events while blocking typing input. The xterm.js frontend handler should forward mouse escape sequences in VIEW mode specifically for scrolling, while still preventing text input. Previously VIEW mode dropped all input indiscriminately.

Tmux alternate screen buffer (used in web UI sessions) requires explicit 'mouse on' mode configured in src/gobby/servers/websocket/tmux.py to handle mouse wheel events. Local scrollback is unavailable in alternate screen—tmux must be configured to capture mouse input directly.

Savings tracking system: savings_ledger table stores individual events, savings_daily stores rolled-up aggregates. SavingsTracker in src/gobby/savings/tracker.py. record.py provides best-effort recording via app_context for MCP tools. CostCalculator in src/gobby/sessions/cost_calculator.py uses ModelCost NamedTuple (input, output, cache_read, cache_creation) from ModelCostStore. cost_table.py init() must unpack ModelCost fields, not tuples. Migration v149 added cache pricing columns + savings tables.
AI/editor tool configuration directories (e.g., `.bmad/`, `.agents/`, `.cursor/`) should be grouped together in `.gitignore` for organizational consistency. `.bmad/` is already present at line 219.

Codex adapter #9987: PRE_COMPACT hook mapped to None in events.py:240 EVENT_TYPE_CLI_SUPPORT, unlike other CLIs (Claude, Gemini, Cursor, Windsurf) which support it. Codex app-server v2 sends contextCompaction type in item/completed events, but EVENT_MAP blanket routes all item/completed → AFTER_TOOL without checking item type. Fix requires conditional routing: inspect item type and route contextCompaction items to PRE_COMPACT hook.

Codex adapter #9986: handle_approval_request() in src/gobby/adapters/codex_impl/adapter.py (lines 245-272) returns minimal {"decision": "accept"|"decline"} responses. Codex supports richer types: acceptForSession, cancel, acceptWithExecpolicyAmendment. HookResponse.auto_approve maps to acceptForSession; decision enum "block" maps to cancel. Enhancement targets translate_from_hook_response() to leverage existing model fields.

BMAD-METHOD uses three-level specialization for task decomposition: Epics (PM agent - feature groupings from PRD with requirements tracing), Stories (Scrum Master agent - implementation-ready units with BDD acceptance criteria and guardrails/learnings from prior work), Tasks (Developer agent - atomic red-green-refactor steps). Role-specific decomposition naturally encodes domain constraints at each level.

Gobby task expansion uses a two-phase pipeline: (1) Research phase: Expander agent reads codebase and produces JSON spec with subtasks, dependencies, affected files, validation criteria; (2) Execution phase: Pipeline validates spec structure (cycles, coverage) then atomically creates all subtasks with dependency wiring. This separation prevents agents from directly creating malformed tasks.
Session resumption is disabled when agents spawn in clone context - agents always start fresh thread rather than resuming previous conversation context via thread ID.
Root cause: SpawnRequest class missing resume_session_id field. Workaround uses clone_id instead (see comment 'Reuse clone_id for thread resume'), indicating intentional but incorrect design choice.

Bug in spawn_executor.py:472: CodexAutonomousRunner.__init__() receives clone_id (git clone resource UUID from clones table) as resume_session_id parameter, but should receive Codex API thread ID instead. Codex API rejects the malformed UUID and silently falls back to new thread creation.

#9987 Codex context compaction hook mapping: Map Codex `contextCompaction` item type to `PRE_COMPACT` HookEvent, active when app-server v2 connection is enabled; handle item/completed event for that type

#9986 CodexAdapter approval response enrichment: Extend adapter to support three richer response types: `acceptForSession` (accept + suppress future prompts in session), `cancel` (cancel operation), `acceptWithExecpolicyAmendment` (accept with execution policy update)
Gobby's expansion pipeline enforces atomic subtask creation - prevents partial states by validating the entire JSON spec from research phase before any subtask creation begins in execution phase

Gobby's task expansion system uses a two-phase pipeline architecture with hard phase boundary: (1) Research phase - Expander agent explores codebase and produces JSON spec containing subtasks with detailed definitions, dependency relationships, affected files list, and validation criteria; (2) Execution phase - Pipeline validates spec (structure, cycles, coverage) then atomically creates all subtasks with wired dependencies

BMAD-METHOD is an external open-source project (https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) referenced as inspiration in Gobby's planning docs, but not bundled or directly integrated into the codebase.

When closing a task that was routed to review (needs_review status), you cannot directly close it. You must first approve it (mark_task_review_approved) then close it. The state machine enforces needs_review → review_approved → closed transitions.
.github/ directory should NOT be ignored in .gitignore as it contains CI workflows and PR templates
AI tool config directories in .gitignore: .claude/, .codex/, .gemini/, .agent/, .agentvibes/, .antigravity/, .copilot/, .windsurf/, .agents/, .cursor/, .bmad/, .quint/

consecutive-tool-block in rule_engine.py (lines 102-124) treats ALL MCP calls as the same tool because it compares event.data.tool_name which is always 'mcp__gobby__call_tool' (the wrapper). Once any MCP call fails 3x, ALL MCP calls are blocked — kill_agent, get_task, everything. The counter only resets when a different NATIVE tool succeeds or on BEFORE_AGENT (session start only, not between agent messages). Fix: use _get_tool_identity() helper that extracts server:tool from tool_input for MCP calls. Lines to fix: 106 (check), 172 (step block), 314 (rule block).
Daily savings aggregation happens via savings_rollup_loop in runner_maintenance.py - system records individual savings events to ledger then rolls up to daily snapshots, not real-time aggregation

Savings tracking integrated across multiple contexts with specific token baselines: handoff system records 15K token baseline vs actual content, memory recall records 8K token baseline vs recalled content - these are design constants affecting savings calculations

CostCalculator (sessions/cost_calculator.py) uses Anthropic ratio fallbacks when LiteLLM registry lacks cache pricing data - this is critical for understanding why cost calculations work when cache prices are missing

Savings tracking uses two-table architecture: savings_ledger records granular events (file compression, handoff baseline), savings_daily aggregates daily totals; SavingsTracker class in savings/tracker.py provides event recording and time-windowed queries; exposed via GET/POST /api/admin/savings

Migration v149 added cache_read_cost_per_token and cache_creation_cost_per_token columns to model_costs table; this is BASELINE_VERSION so ensure audit when bumping version includes these columns in baseline table definition (src/gobby/storage/migrations.py)

Cache pricing is extracted from the LiteLLM registry in ModelCostStore rather than hardcoded; CostCalculator uses cache_read_cost_per_token and cache_creation_cost_per_token from LiteLLM with Anthropic ratio fallbacks for missing transcript cost data (src/gobby/sessions/cost_calculator.py, lifecycle.py)
Pre-commit hook 'require-error-triage' only triggers when Python files are modified. Changes to .gitignore alone do not require lint/test verification
.github/ directory should NOT be in .gitignore because it contains CI workflows and PR templates that must be version-controlled

Agent_runs table extended in migration v147 with five new columns: pid, tmux_session_name, mode, worktree_id, clone_id. These track process lifecycle state for agent recovery and cleanup logic. BASELINE_SCHEMA kept in sync per migration system requirements.

CodexAutonomousRunner session ID threading bug: passing request.clone_id (git clone UUID) as resume_session_id parameter causes session management mismatch. Correct approach: capture Codex thread IDs post-execution via update_sdk_session_id(), not from request.clone_id. Fresh autonomous spawns should not reuse thread IDs from previous runs. This affects cross-mode resume operations. Fixed in commit 80565d54.

Fixed in commit 80565d54: SpawnRequest.clone_id was incorrectly passed as resume_session_id to CodexAutonomousRunner. clone_id is a git clone UUID, not a Codex thread ID. Fix: removed the parameter to match Claude autonomous pattern (fresh spawns start fresh threads). Thread IDs are already captured post-run via agent_run_manager.update_sdk_session_id() for future cross-mode resume.

The memory-lifecycle workflow rules enforce a requirement for the hook context variable `pending_memory_review` (checked via `variables.get('pending_memory_review')`). This is defined in `src/gobby/install/shared_workflows/memory-lifecycle.yaml`. Hook failures related to memory lifecycle rules are typically due to this variable not being set.

Parallel rules pattern (same enforcement concern via different event types) is an architectural smell. Plan-mode conditional logic (not variables.get('plan_mode')) cannot fix core architectural mismatch - the real solution is enforcing at the correct event/tool trigger point, not adding conditions to wrong trigger.

require-memory-review stop-gate rule was soft-deleted (not source value changed) because enforcement should only occur at close_task tool trigger, never at session stop. Two parallel rules existed: require-memory-review (stop event, was incorrect) and require-memory-review-before-close (close event, is correct). Memory review gate belongs on task close lifecycle, not session lifecycle.

When spawning fresh autonomous runs in CodexAutonomousRunner, capture the actual Codex thread ID post-run using update_sdk_session_id() for cross-mode resume capability. Fresh spawns create fresh threads and should not reuse previous session IDs.

In CodexAutonomousRunner, do not pass request.clone_id (git clone UUID) as the resume_session_id parameter. The resume_session_id must be a Codex thread ID, not a git operation identifier. Commit 80565d54 fixed this bug by removing the incorrect parameter assignment.
`item/completed` events with `type: "contextCompaction"` route to `PRE_COMPACT` handler instead of being dropped (task #9987)
`translate_from_hook_response` now maps all 5 Codex decision types: block→cancel, auto_approve→acceptForSession, exec_policy_amendment mapping added (commit 037357fb, task #9986)
item/completed events with type='contextCompaction' must route to PRE_COMPACT state (not silently drop) to enable compaction rules like handoff injection and output compression for Codex sessions
Codex hook response decision type mappings in translate_from_hook_response: block→cancel, auto_approve→acceptForSession, exec_policy_amendment→acceptWithExecpolicyAmendment

CodexAdapter approval responses: block→cancel, auto_approve→acceptForSession, exec_policy_amendment metadata→acceptWithExecpolicyAmendment. Context compaction: item/completed with type=contextCompaction routes to PRE_COMPACT. EVENT_TYPE_CLI_SUPPORT updated to show codex supports PRE_COMPACT via contextCompaction.

`require-memory-review` (stop-gate rule) was deleted and existed only as a DB-installed rule—no template YAML file was ever created for it. The active memory review enforcement is now exclusively via `require-memory-review-before-close.yaml` (close-task gate, located in memory-lifecycle/). These are two distinct rules.

When workflows transition between steps (e.g., 'implement' → 'terminate'), reset consecutive_tool_blocks, _last_blocked_tool, and tool_block_pending counters to prevent failure state from bleeding across step boundaries.

_recover_task_from_failed_agent() uses 3-tier recovery strategy: (1) DB record lookup, (2) registry task_id, (3) assignee-based lookup. Addresses critical gap where agents could lose task context mid-execution if registration failed, causing orphaned agents unable to recover their assigned task.

In rule_engine.py, _get_tool_identity() tracks MCP tool calls using 'server:tool' format (e.g., 'gobby-tasks:mark_task_needs_review') instead of generic 'mcp__gobby__call_tool'. This prevents cascading failures where one MCP tool blocks subsequent calls.

Dev/QA agents already have allowed_tools: 'all' configured, which enables gobby-memory:create_memory calls without additional agent configuration changes. File: agent configuration (project-specific).

Stop-gates should not gate stop events globally. The require-memory-review gate was incorrectly blocking all stop events; it should only gate specific transitions (close_task, mark_task_needs_review, mark_task_review_approved) when pending_memory_review status is set. Gate scope must be transition-specific, not event-type-broad.

Two new granular gate rules added to `require-memory-review-before-close.yaml`: (1) `require-memory-review-before-needs-review` gates `mark_task_needs_review` on `pending_memory_review` condition; (2) `require-memory-review-before-approve` gates `mark_task_review_approved` on `pending_memory_review` condition. These replace the overly broad `require-memory-review` rule.

Hard-deleted the `require-memory-review` stop-gate rule from database because it was incorrectly gatekeeping `stop_task` operation. Memory review gates should only control state transitions that require memory context, not stop events.

Task ID propagation through agent lifecycle: to_dict() method includes task_id serialization for agent state persistence; recover_or_cleanup_agents() function passes task_id parameter when re-registering recovered agents to maintain task context downstream.

Rule engine test suite has 2 pre-existing test failures related to validation model test expectations. These failures predate the agent task_id propagation work and are safe to ignore in future sessions.

mark_needs_review and review_approved agents carry tag:default, making automation rules track-pending-memory-review and clear-memory-review-on-create automatically applicable to their memory operations.

consecutive-tool-block in rule_engine.py treated ALL MCP calls as the same tool because it compared event.data.tool_name which is always 'mcp__gobby__call_tool'. FIXED in commit dfe71d70: _get_tool_identity() helper extracts 'server:tool' from tool_input for MCP calls. Also resets consecutive-tool-block counters on step transitions (in _process_step_after_tool) so failures from implement don't bleed into terminate.

Task-Expander Pipeline: 9-stage architecture: (1) gather_context, (2-5) review gates (skippable), (6) final gate, (7) epic expansion, (8) child epic expansion (parallel), (9) reconciliation with three operations (verify, auto-fix, escalate unresolvable). Documented in docs/plans/task-expander-pipeline-draft.md

When daemon is unavailable, spawned agents should halt execution (block tools) to prevent operation without safety constraints. Interactive users should proceed normally. This asymmetry requires execution context discrimination via GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID env var in hook dispatcher.

Hook dispatcher captures GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID from environment (line 229). Spawned agents have this env var set; interactive sessions don't. Use os.environ.get('GOBBY_AGENT_RUN_ID') to discriminate between execution contexts for conditional tool blocking.

Daemon restart vulnerability: hook_dispatcher.py:517 contains stderr message suggesting 'gobby start' that gets redirected by Claude Code hooks back to spawned agents. Cloned environments misinterpret this diagnostic message as executable directive, spawning unintended daemons in feedback loop.

TRUST_PROMPT_PATTERNS constant requires updates when Claude changes trust-prompt output format; add new regex patterns for variations (recent example: 'Is this a project you created or one you trust')

Trust-prompt dismiss key changed from '2\n' to '\n' because option-2 selection inadvertently approved parent directories; single newline (default confirmation) prevents scope creep while fallback detector handles variations

Safety rule protection for spawned agents is enforced via tag-based rule selection: `pipeline-worker`, `developer`, and `qa-reviewer` agents all include `tag:worker-safety` matching. Auto-installation via `install_all_templates(tag="gobby")` in `cli/installers/shared.py` triggers both installation and enablement of templates carrying the gobby tag, per migration v125.

Trust prompt detector (src/gobby/agents/prompt_detector.py): TRUST_DISMISS_KEYS must be '\n' (Enter only), NOT '2\n'. Option 2 is 'Trust parent Folder' which trusts the parent directory — dangerous when parallel dev pipelines use sibling clones. TRUST_PROMPT_PATTERNS has 3 patterns: 'Do you trust the files' (old Claude), 'Is this a project you created or one you trust' (new Claude), and 'Trust.*Folder'. Lifecycle monitor (check_trust_prompts) uses these to auto-dismiss within 30s.
Issues #10054 and #10055 cover trust prompt detection for Claude Code workspaces - both were already fully implemented with test coverage when investigated, pending closure confirmation

Trust prompt detection uses three-layer defense: (1) Pre-approval in src/gobby/agents/trust.py creates ~/.claude/projects/ directory entries before workspace spawn to prevent prompts initially, (2) Runtime detection in src/gobby/agents/prompt_detector.py monitors tmux output for trust prompt patterns and responds with '\n' (Enter only), (3) Lifecycle integration in src/gobby/agents/lifecycle_monitor.py executes detection checks every 30 seconds

task_selection_history is NOT redundant with session_tasks: it records every timestamp a task is selected (no deduplication) for temporal analysis. Primary use case is stuck task detector. Serves distinct purpose from session_tasks' relationship-type tracking.

session_tasks table has UNIQUE(session_id, task_id, action) constraint - each action type (claimed, created, closed, worked_on, etc.) appears once per session-task pair, providing deduplicated relationship tracking. Used across 16 files as operational ledger for session-to-task relationships.

Schema cleanup pattern: 0% data population ≠ dead code. Must cross-reference columns against active code before deletion. Examples of untriggered but sound features to KEEP: compacted_at (compaction feature exists), complexity_score, estimated_subtasks (CLI scoring reads/writes). Only drop if both data and code references are absent.

External validator cleanup scope: Remove use_external_validator column from tasks table, delete external_validator.py + tests, remove ~7 config references across ~10 files. Feature code exists but task code never actually calls it.

Dead columns in tasks table ready to drop (0% data + no code refs): summary, original_instruction, details, verification, agent_name, reference_doc. Identified by reconciling data population against code dependencies.

GitHub/Linear integration code spans 7 files across 4 modules requiring coordinated cleanup: src/gobby/integrations/{github,linear}.py, src/gobby/sync/{github,linear}.py, src/gobby/cli/{github,linear}.py, src/gobby/servers/routes/source_control.py. Affects 5 task columns.

GitHub/Linear integration in Gobby is non-functional—code exists and imports successfully but runtime functionality is disabled (0% task column population). Appears to be incomplete removal or deprecated-but-persisted code rather than active feature.

Daemon safety hardening: Hook dispatcher blocks ALL tools for spawned agents when daemon unavailable, enabling graceful shutdown. Interactive sessions unchanged. Security rule no-daemon-management-http blocks curl commands to admin restart/shutdown endpoints. Commit 7205d6a8.
RuleDefinitionBody API enforces exclusive use of effects=[...] array syntax. Deprecated singular effect= parameter removed. ~70 test callsites updated across 6 files. Commit fc9003f6.

Migration v150 removed SQLite transcript storage. Deleted components: LocalSessionTranscriptManager class, TranscriptSnapshotThrottle class, session_transcripts database table. Transcripts now stored on filesystem at ~/.gobby/session_transcripts/. HTTP endpoints, MCP tools, and CLI updated to use new location.

session_transcripts table was dropped in migration v150. Transcript storage is now filesystem-only via transcript_archive.py (~/.gobby/session_transcripts/). No more DB blob storage. BASELINE_VERSION is now 150.

Internal LLM validation (task_validator.validate_task() at src/gobby/storage/ line 212) executes mandatorily on every task close. External validation (run_external_validation() line 238) only executes when TaskValidationConfig.use_external_validator=True, which has never been enabled in practice.

tasks.use_external_validator column is 0% populated and unused for control flow—represents technical debt. Candidate for removal in future migration after config-level flag becomes primary design pattern.
